# Polaris Documentation

> User and developer documentation for Polaris Finance — the self-scaling stablecoin operating system. Mint pAssets backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield. No T-bills. No CEXs. No compromises.

## Agent Guidance

- Start with [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) for what is live, supported, or production-final.
- Treat [Public Testnet 1](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet) as the source of truth for Sepolia network, parameter, and contract-address values.
- Public Testnet 1 assets are Sepolia test assets with no monetary value, no production redemption, and no mainnet claim.
- Do not infer production addresses, production constants, audit status, or launch status from testnet values.
- Prefer the Markdown URLs in this file for retrieval, and cite the canonical URL shown beside each page.

## Polaris Finance Documentation

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/index.md

Polaris sustains a yield layer for all of DeFi to seize, centered on **pETH**, a tailor-made collateral for Polaris CDPs that also has a life of its own.

- **pETH** with the bonding curve, its self-scaling liquidity source ensuring strong constant liquidity
- **pUSD**, **pGOLD** and other **pAssets** use it as collateral to back their stablecoin
- A tranching layer splitting **pETH** into **fpETH** for protected yield exposure, and **vpETH** for maximal upside with no downside protection
- The protocol is immutable at its core, and stewarded by the **POLAR** lockers

Polaris is a trustless and immutable infrastructure for minting synthetic assets (**pAssets**) backed entirely by onchain collateral and yield. It provides competitive risk-adjusted alternative yield sources for both ETH and stablecoins. Flows enable any third-party protocol to build on top of Polaris assets by apportioning yield flows based on contract token ownership.

This documentation covers how the system works, how to use the app, and which risks to check before signing. For current network, contract, and actionability status, start with [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status).

## Launch timeline

Image: Polaris launch timeline. Early Research in 2024. Team Formation in June 2025. Public Testnet 1 on Sepolia in March 2026, the current phase. Mainnet, forthcoming.

Image: Polaris system overview: ETH swaps into pETH on the bonding curve, pETH collateralizes CDPs that mint pUSD, and burning pETH for POLAR raises the floor and releases ETH.

## The three engines

Polaris has three engines:

Engine | What it is | Why it matters

**1. pETH** | ETH deposited into a two-way **bonding curve**, with a contract-defined floor under stated assumptions | A capital-efficient, yield-bearing collateral with a contract-defined exit path
**2. pAssets** | Synthetic pegged (USD, Gold, etc.) assets minted against collateral, via a Collateralized Debt Position (CDP) | Counterparty-free stables that scale with onchain activity
**3. POLAR** | The stewardship token, minted only by burning pETH in a **conversion auction** | Aligns long-term incentives and steers the protocol within hardcoded bounds

Relevant app/search vocabulary: app, official app, testnet, Public Testnet 1, Sepolia, pETH, pAssets.

---

## Polaris Launch Status

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status.md

This page owns current status: what is live, what is testnet-only, what is not live, and which app URL is official.

## Current phase

Item | Status

Public phase | **Public Testnet 1**
Testnet network | **Sepolia**, chain ID `11155111`
Official testnet app | [https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/)
Testnet-active pAssets | pUSD and pGOLD
Illustrative or future pAssets | pCHF, pBigMac, and other catalog examples
Production network | Ethereum mainnet, chain ID `1`, forthcoming

## What this means

- You can try Polaris on Public Testnet 1 with Sepolia test assets only.
- Testnet assets have no monetary value, no production redemption, and no mainnet claim.
- Do not use a production app, production address, audit claim, bounty link, or mainnet workflow unless this page says it is live.
- Current Sepolia artifacts can change after redeploys.

## Where each exact fact lives

Need | Canonical page

Fastest safe testnet flow | [Public Testnet Quickstart](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/quickstart)
Sepolia manifest paths and artifact sources | [Public Testnet 1 Artifacts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet)

Audit and disclosure status | [Audits & Security](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/audits-security)

## Start here

If you want to use the app now, follow [Public Testnet Quickstart](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/quickstart). If anything in your wallet prompt conflicts with this page, stop and verify before signing.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: app, official app, testnet app, app.testnet.polarisfinance.io, connect wallet, testnet, Public Testnet 1, Sepolia, Sepolia ETH, chain ID 11155111, WETH faucet, borrow, earn, swap, contracts, audits.

---

## Public Testnet Quickstart

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/quickstart
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/quickstart.md

Use this page for the shortest safe path from a wallet to a first Polaris testnet action. It covers Public Testnet 1 only.

## Before you start

- A wallet you can connect to Sepolia.
- Sepolia ETH for gas.
- The official Public Testnet 1 app URL: [https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/).
- Test WETH from the app faucet: pay `0.1` Sepolia ETH, receive `10` WETH.

Do not send mainnet ETH, mainnet WETH, or production assets to testnet contracts.

## Steps

1. Open [app.testnet.polarisfinance.io](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/).
2. Connect your wallet on Sepolia, chain ID `11155111`.
3. Get Sepolia ETH for gas from a Sepolia faucet if needed.
4. Claim test WETH from the on-Dashboard faucet (pay `0.1` Sepolia ETH, receive `10` WETH).
5. Use [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap) to buy pETH on the bonding curve.
6. Use [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) to mint pUSD or pGOLD against pETH.
7. Optionally use [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn) or [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap) to deposit a pAsset into the Stability Pool.
8. Return to the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) and confirm your positions and rewards.

## Success checks

Check | Expected result

Network | Wallet shows Sepolia, chain ID `11155111`
Frontend | URL is exactly `https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io/`
Assets | Balances are testnet assets only
pETH | pETH balance updates after swap
Borrow | pUSD or pGOLD position appears after minting
Earn | Stability Pool deposit appears after deposit or zap

## Next actions

- [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting) if balances, quotes, approvals, or transactions fail.
- [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) for current testnet values.
- [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) before treating any testnet behavior as production guidance.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: quickstart, troubleshooting, wrong network, Sepolia, faucet, approval, stale quote, failed transaction.

---

## Dashboard

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app.md

## What it does

The Dashboard ("Your Polaris") is your starting point and onboarding hub. It shows current Stability Pool and staking APRs, a three-step path to your first position (connect a wallet → claim test WETH from the faucet → earn pUSD), an fpETH long-term-yield shortcut, and a live price ticker for pETH, pUSD, pGOLD, and POLAR. The **Activity** panel logs what you do this session (local only). For position detail use [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) and [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn); for protocol-wide charts open **Analytics**, a separate tab.

Image: Polaris Dashboard ("Your Polaris") showing Stability Pool and staking APR cards, the connect-faucet-earn onboarding steps, and the token price ticker

*The Dashboard is the onboarding and overview screen, not a detailed positions table.*

## Before you start

- Use Public Testnet 1 at [app.testnet.polarisfinance.io](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io).
- Connect to Sepolia, chain ID `11155111`, and hold Sepolia ETH for gas.
- Claim test WETH from the on-Dashboard faucet: pay `0.1` Sepolia ETH, receive `10` WETH. One claim per address every 4 hours, subject to a daily cap.
- Confirm the frontend, network, and wallet prompts against [Verification](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#verification). Use test assets only — see [Public Testnet 1 Artifacts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet).

## Steps/checks

1. Connect your wallet on Sepolia (STEP 1).
2. Claim test WETH from the faucet (STEP 2).
3. Review the APR cards for the pUSD and pGOLD Stability Pools and POLAR staking.
4. Start a first position from STEP 3 (earn pUSD) or the fpETH yield card, or move to an action tab.
5. Watch the **Activity** panel for this session's transactions, and the ticker for live prices.
6. Open **Analytics** for protocol-wide charts, then return to the relevant action tab for a fresh quote.

The Dashboard is current when the connected wallet, Sepolia network, APRs, and prices match what you expect.

## Common failures

- Wallet is on the wrong chain.
- Faucet claim is blocked by the per-address cooldown or the daily cap.
- APR or price values change because protocol state moved; refresh before acting.
- A position is not reflected because it belongs to another wallet.

## Risks/links

Dashboard values are state reads, not final transaction terms. Use the action tab quote before signing. The Activity panel is local and session-only. For canonical status, risks, and addresses, use [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status), [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure), and [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#contracts-and-addresses).

## Next actions

- Use [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap) to buy pETH on the bonding curve.
- Use [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) to open and manage debt positions.
- Use [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn) to supply a Stability Pool or stake POLAR or vpETH.
- See the in-app **Guide** for a by-token map of what to do.
- Use [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting) if wallet state, quotes, or balances look wrong.

---

## Advanced Tools in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/advanced
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/advanced.md

## What it does

Advanced holds expert, permissionless operations aimed at arbitrageurs and keepers — deeper mechanism knowledge is assumed. It has three modules:

- **Peg Stability Module (PSM):** mint or redeem pUSD or pGOLD against pETH at the oracle price, in unlimited size. Mint sends pETH in for the pAsset at peg; redeem sends the pAsset in for pETH at peg. This is the arbitrage that anchors each peg.
- **Convert pETH → POLAR:** a one-way auction. pETH is burned (raising the bonding-curve floor) and POLAR is minted at the decaying auction price. There is no reverse direction; in practice this is run by MEV/arbitrage bots.
- **Testnet ETH Faucet:** pay `0.1` Sepolia ETH, receive `10` WETH. One claim per address every 4 hours, subject to a daily cap.

Most users never need this tab — the guided tabs cover normal flows.

## Before you start

- Start from the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) and verify the current app and network first.
- Prefer the guided tabs ([Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow), [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn), [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap), [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split), [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap)) when they support your action.
- For PSM and conversion, understand that profit only exists when the market price is off the peg or auction price; otherwise the round-trip loses fees.
- Verify the contract, token, spender, and amount before signing. Keep [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#contracts-and-addresses) open.

## Steps/checks

1. Pick the module: PSM mint/redeem, pETH → POLAR conversion, or faucet.
2. For PSM, choose pUSD or pGOLD and the direction (mint or redeem); review the output and fee at the oracle price.
3. For conversion, review the current auction price against the market POLAR price before converting.
4. Approve the input token if required, then sign.
5. Confirm the resulting balance and that no unexpected allowance remains.

The action worked when the intended balance changed and no unexpected allowance or position remains.

## Common failures

- Market price is on peg (or below the auction price), so the round-trip is unprofitable.
- Approval was signed but the action transaction was not.
- Wrong pAsset or direction selected in the PSM.
- Faucet claim is blocked by the per-address cooldown or daily cap.
- Wallet warning indicates an unexpected contract or permission.

## Risks/links

Advanced operations assume you understand the mechanism. PSM and conversion are permissionless but unforgiving of mistakes. Use [Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations), [pAsset Catalog](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog), [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions), [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics), and [Public Testnet 1 Artifacts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet) as the canonical references.

## Next actions

- Return to the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) to confirm state.
- Use the guided [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow), [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn), [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split), or [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap) page when possible.
- Use [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting) before retrying a failed transaction.

---

## Borrow in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow.md

## What it does

Borrow is where you open and manage Polaris debt positions. It has three sub-tabs:

- **pUSD** and **pGOLD:** deposit **pETH** collateral and mint an overcollateralized stablecoin (a CDP, also called a trove). You set collateral and debt with an LTV slider and see a projected liquidation price, estimated monthly cost, and effective rate before signing.
- **ETH:** a reserve loan — borrow **ETH** against **fpETH** collateral at up to 100% LTV with no liquidation risk, because the fpETH floor only ever rises.

Existing positions are adjusted, repaid, and closed from this same tab once your wallet is connected.

Image: Polaris Borrow tab with pUSD, pGOLD, and ETH sub-tabs, a collateral field, LTV slider, effective rate, and a projected-position panel

*Borrow opens and manages positions; the projected-position panel previews liquidation price, monthly cost, and LTV before you sign.*

## Before you start

- Start from the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) and verify the current app and network first.
- Hold the collateral (pETH for pUSD/pGOLD, fpETH for the ETH loan) plus Sepolia ETH for gas.
- For pUSD/pGOLD, understand that the position carries liquidation, redemption, and Recovery Mode exposure. The ETH/fpETH reserve loan is not liquidated.
- Review [Open a Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove) for the full pAsset minting flow.

## Steps/checks

1. Choose the sub-tab: pUSD, pGOLD, or ETH.
2. Enter collateral and set the debt with the LTV slider (25%, 50%, 75% presets are available).
3. Review the projected liquidation price, estimated monthly cost, effective rate, and LTV.
4. Approve the collateral token if prompted, then sign.
5. To manage an existing position, reopen it in this tab and add collateral, mint more, repay, withdraw collateral, or close — review the new LTV first.
6. Confirm the updated collateral, debt, and LTV.

A borrow or management action worked when the position reflects the expected collateral, debt, and LTV.

## Common failures

- LTV exceeds the allowed threshold for the position.
- Debt amount is too small or too large for the current collateral.
- Recovery Mode blocks a risk-increasing action.
- Price movement changes projected health before inclusion.
- Repay amount exceeds your pAsset balance.
- You are connected with a wallet that does not own the position.

## Risks/links

Borrowing pUSD or pGOLD creates liquidation and redemption exposure; a low LTV protects against liquidation, not against proportional redemptions. The ETH/fpETH reserve loan avoids liquidation but ties up fpETH. Keep protocol explanations in [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting), [Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations), [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations), and [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode). Use [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) for current values and [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) for risks.

## Next actions

- Use [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap) to acquire pETH collateral, or [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split) to get fpETH for an ETH loan.
- Use [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn) to deposit a minted pAsset into its Stability Pool.
- See the in-app **Guide** for borrow-based strategies such as Floor Carry and collateral-only CDPs.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Borrow, Manage Trove, borrow, mint, open trove, repay, close trove, pUSD, pGOLD, LTV, loan-to-value, official app.

---

## Earn in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn.md

## What it does

Earn groups Polaris's yield positions across four sub-tabs:

- **pUSD** and **pGOLD** Stability Pools: deposit the pAsset to backstop liquidations; earn pro-rata pAsset interest plus pETH liquidation gains.
- **POLAR** staking: earn a pro-rata share of borrower interest paid in pUSD and pGOLD, plus governance weight.
- **vpETH** staking: earn a pETH stream from the bonding curve — higher risk, amplified by protocol activity.

Each sub-tab shows its current APR, a deposit/withdraw (or stake/unstake) control, and an option to claim pending rewards in the same transaction.

Image: Polaris Earn tab with pUSD, pGOLD, POLAR, and vpETH sub-tabs, an APR, a deposit/withdraw toggle, and a claim-pending option

*Earn groups Stability Pool and staking positions; each sub-tab shows its APR and a claim-pending option.*

## Before you start

- Start from the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) and verify the current app and network first.
- Hold the deposit token (the pAsset, POLAR, or vpETH) and Sepolia ETH for gas.
- Decide which sub-tab you want: a Stability Pool, POLAR staking, or vpETH staking.
- Read [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield) before depositing.

## Steps/checks

1. Select the sub-tab and review its APR, your current deposit, and pending gains.
2. Choose deposit/withdraw (or stake/unstake).
3. Leave "Also claim pending …" checked to claim rewards in the same transaction, or uncheck it.
4. Approve the token if required, then sign.
5. Confirm your deposit, balance, and claimable gains updated.

The action worked when the Earn card and wallet balances reflect the transaction.

## Common failures

- Deposit token balance is too low.
- Approval was signed but the action transaction was not.
- Pending rewards are dust-sized or not yet claimable.
- APR changed after refresh.
- Wallet is connected to the wrong account.

## Risks/links

Stability Pool deposits are first-loss capital during liquidations. vpETH staking is the highest-risk Earn position. APRs are shown live but are not guaranteed returns. Use [Yield Sources](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources), [pETH Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) (for vpETH), [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics), and [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) for the underlying risk model.

## Next actions

- Use [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap) or [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split) to acquire deposit assets.
- Use [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap) for a bundled swap-plus-deposit.
- Use the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) to monitor deposits and rewards.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Stability Pool, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Split

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split.md

## What it does

Split converts pETH into matching fpETH and vpETH at a fixed **1:1:1** ratio — no fees, no oracle, and fully reversible: merge 1 fpETH + 1 vpETH back into 1 pETH at any time. **fpETH** is the floor leg, redeemable for the bonding curve's floor price (which only ever rises), so it gains in ETH terms with little volatility. **vpETH** is the above-floor leg, absorbing all of pETH's volatility above the floor. Use this page for the workflow; use [pETH Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) for the mechanism.

Image: Polaris Split tab with a Split/Merge toggle, fpETH and vpETH outputs, live floor and market prices, and a price simulator

*Split and Merge change your pETH exposure; the "What if…" simulator shows how each leg responds to price moves.*

## Before you start

- Start from the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) and verify the current app and network first.
- Hold pETH for splitting, or equal fpETH and vpETH amounts for merging.
- Confirm which leg you intend to hold after the action — they behave very differently.

## Steps/checks

1. Choose Split or Merge.
2. Enter the amount and review the expected fpETH + vpETH (or pETH) output.
3. Approve pETH, fpETH, or vpETH if required.
4. Sign the transaction.
5. Confirm wallet balances update.

A split worked when pETH decreases and fpETH plus vpETH each increase by the same amount. A merge worked when equal fpETH and vpETH decrease and pETH increases.

## Common failures

- Merge amount exceeds the smaller of your fpETH or vpETH balances.
- Token balance exists but is hidden in the wallet UI.
- Approval was granted for one leg but not the other.
- You expected holding one leg to behave like holding unsplit pETH.

## Risks/links

Holding only fpETH or only vpETH changes your exposure: fpETH tracks the floor with little volatility, while vpETH amplifies upside and downside. Read [pETH Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split), [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price), and [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) before relying on either leg.

## Next actions

- Use [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) (the ETH sub-tab) to borrow ETH against fpETH.
- Use [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn) to stake vpETH for a pETH yield stream.
- Merge in this tab to recombine the legs into pETH.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price.

---

## Swap in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap.md

## What it does

Swap is the bonding-curve interface for pETH. Sell ETH (WETH) to **buy** pETH, or sell pETH back for ETH — the route is always `WETH → bonding curve → pETH`. Each mint locks ETH into the curve's reserve at the current marginal price, and pETH carries a hard, ever-rising price floor. To get fpETH or vpETH use [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split); to mint pUSD or pGOLD use [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow).

Image: Polaris Swap tab showing the Sell/Buy direction, the WETH to pETH bonding-curve route, the quote, and slippage controls

*Swap routes WETH through the bonding curve to pETH; refresh and review the quote before signing.*

## Before you start

- Start from the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) and verify the current app and network first.
- Hold WETH (claim it from the faucet) and enough Sepolia ETH for gas.
- Read the direction, output, minimum received, price impact, and approval target before signing.

## Steps/checks

1. Choose the direction: buy pETH with WETH, or sell pETH for WETH.
2. Enter the amount and review the quote and route.
3. Check minimum received, price impact, fees, and slippage.
4. Approve the input token only if the spender matches the app flow.
5. Sign the swap transaction.
6. Confirm the pETH (or WETH) balance updates in the wallet.

The swap worked when the transaction lands and the output token balance updates.

## Common failures

- Quote expired before the transaction landed.
- Slippage limit was too tight.
- Input balance or allowance was too low.
- Gas was too low or Sepolia was congested.
- Wallet is connected to the wrong account or network.

## Risks/links

Swaps can lose value through slippage, price movement, stale quotes, and gas. pETH mechanics are explained in [pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth) and [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price). Use [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) for risk details.

## Next actions

- Use [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split) to convert pETH into fpETH and vpETH.
- Use [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) to mint pUSD or pGOLD against pETH.
- Use [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap) if you want the app to bundle a swap with a deposit.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price.

---

## Zap in the Polaris App

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap.md

## What it does

Zap bundles several on-chain steps into one guided transaction. Pick a strategy:

- **pUSD Stable Yield:** market-buys pUSD with your WETH and deposits it into the pUSD Stability Pool in a single transaction, earning SP yield from CDP interest plus liquidation gains.
- **Floor Carry:** converts ETH into fpETH and borrows ETH against it — a non-liquidatable, loopable ETH-yield position.

You stay the position owner with full self-custody, slippage is bounded by your slippage setting, and a simulation confirms the expected result before you sign. A zap is an execution shortcut — it doesn't change the economics of the underlying steps, and you can always exit or manage the resulting position from Earn or Borrow.

Image: Polaris Zap tab showing the pUSD Stable Yield route from WETH through pUSD into the Stability Pool

*The pUSD Stable Yield zap market-buys pUSD with WETH and deposits it into the Stability Pool in one transaction.*

## Before you start

- Start from the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) and verify the current app and network first.
- Hold the input asset (WETH or ETH) and Sepolia ETH for gas.
- Read the full route before signing.
- Be prepared to finish manually through Swap, Earn, or Borrow if a bundled step fails.

## Steps/checks

1. Choose the strategy.
2. Enter the input amount.
3. Review every route leg, the output estimate, slippage, and the deposit or loan target.
4. Let the simulation confirm the expected output.
5. Sign approvals only for expected spenders, then sign the zap.
6. Confirm the final position on the Dashboard, Earn, or Borrow tab.

The zap worked when the final target position appears, not merely when the first approval succeeds.

## Common failures

- One leg became stale before inclusion.
- Slippage or liquidity changed.
- Approval succeeded but the bundled action failed.
- Gas estimate was too low for the full route.
- The wallet now holds an intermediate asset instead of the final position.

## Risks/links

A zap combines swap, approval, and deposit or borrow risks. If a zap stops midway, inspect balances before retrying. Use [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap), [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn), [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow), [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield), and [Troubleshooting](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting) for manual fallback.

## Next actions

- Use the [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) to confirm the final state.
- Use [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap) if you received an intermediate token.
- Use [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn) or [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) to complete or manage the position manually.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Stability Pool, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Troubleshooting

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/troubleshooting.md

Use this page when the app, wallet, quote, balance, or transaction does not behave as expected.

Start from the known-good setup: Public Testnet 1 at [app.testnet.polarisfinance.io](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io), Sepolia chain ID `11155111`, test assets only.

## Wrong network

**Symptom:** The app asks you to switch networks, actions are disabled, or wallet prompts mention another chain.

**Likely cause:** Wallet is not connected to Sepolia.

**Fix:** Switch the wallet network to Sepolia, chain ID `11155111`, then refresh the app.

**When not to proceed:** Do not sign if the wallet prompt shows mainnet or an unexpected chain.

**Related reference:** [Public Testnet 1 Artifacts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet)

## Wrong frontend

**Symptom:** The app looks unfamiliar, links differ, or wallet prompts do not match the expected action.

**Likely cause:** You are on the wrong frontend or a phishing copy.

**Fix:** Use [app.testnet.polarisfinance.io](https://app.testnet.polarisfinance.io) for Public Testnet 1 and verify links from the docs.

**When not to proceed:** Do not connect or sign from an unverified URL.

**Related reference:** [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status)

## Faucet or test-token shortage

**Symptom:** You cannot start because WETH, pETH, pAssets, POLAR, or Sepolia ETH are missing.

**Likely cause:** The wallet lacks test assets or gas.

**Fix:** Follow [Public Testnet Quickstart](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/quickstart), then refresh balances.

**When not to proceed:** Do not bridge or send real mainnet funds to testnet contracts.

**Related reference:** [Public Testnet 1 Artifacts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet)

## Missing balance

**Symptom:** The app shows a balance but the wallet does not, or the wallet shows a token the app does not.

**Likely cause:** Token is hidden in the wallet, the wrong account is connected, or the app state is stale.

**Fix:** Confirm the connected account, refresh the app, and import the token using the canonical address if needed.

**When not to proceed:** Do not import token addresses from chat links or search results.

**Related reference:** [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#contracts-and-addresses)

## Approval succeeded, action still fails

**Symptom:** Wallet shows an approval transaction succeeded, but the app still asks for approval or the main action fails.

**Likely cause:** Approval was for the wrong spender, wrong token, insufficient amount, or a different account.

**Fix:** Check spender, token, wallet account, and allowance. Then retry only from the correct action tab.

**When not to proceed:** Do not grant unlimited approvals to an unexpected spender.

**Related reference:** [Advanced](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/advanced)

## Stale quote or changed output

**Symptom:** Swap or Zap output changes, minimum received differs, or the transaction reverts.

**Likely cause:** Price, liquidity, route, gas, or slippage changed before inclusion.

**Fix:** Refresh the quote, review the route again, and use a slippage setting you understand.

**When not to proceed:** Do not sign if the wallet prompt no longer matches the app quote.

**Related reference:** [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap), [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap)

## Borrow action disabled or reverted

**Symptom:** Borrow, repay, adjust, or close cannot be submitted.

**Likely cause:** Position health, collateral, debt amount, Recovery Mode, allowance, or price movement blocks the action.

**Fix:** Refresh position data, reduce risk, check collateral and debt amounts, and retry from the Borrow tab.

**When not to proceed:** Do not borrow if you do not understand the displayed liquidation exposure.

**Related reference:** [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow), [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode), [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure)

## Earn action unavailable

**Symptom:** Deposit, withdraw, claim, stake, or unstake is disabled.

**Likely cause:** No eligible balance, no claimable amount, current deployment limits, or wallet/network mismatch.

**Fix:** Confirm the selected Earn surface, wallet account, balances, and network.

**When not to proceed:** Do not treat APR estimates as guaranteed returns.

**Related reference:** [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn), [Yield](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield)

## Split or merge fails

**Symptom:** Split or Merge reverts, or Merge allows less than expected.

**Likely cause:** You lack pETH for Split or equal fpETH and vpETH balances for Merge.

**Fix:** Check both token balances and merge only up to the smaller leg balance.

**When not to proceed:** Do not hold only one leg unless you understand the changed exposure.

**Related reference:** [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split), [pETH Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split)

## Transaction pending, out of gas, or reverted

**Symptom:** Wallet shows pending for a long time, or the transaction fails after submission.

**Likely cause:** Sepolia congestion, low gas, state changed, or transaction simulation missed a revert.

**Fix:** Wait for the pending transaction to settle, then refresh app state before retrying.

**When not to proceed:** Do not repeatedly submit the same action without checking whether the first transaction changed balances, allowances, or positions.

**Related reference:** [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app)

Relevant app/search vocabulary: quickstart, troubleshooting, wrong network, Sepolia, faucet, approval, stale quote, failed transaction.

---

## Polaris Core Concepts

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts.md

## The shape of the system

*Key takeaway: one collateral primitive feeds the whole system; pETH creates collateral, CDPs mint pAssets, and POLAR conversion raises the pETH floor.*

The diagram traces four steps and an outcome:

1. **Swap.** ETH enters the **bonding curve** and is issued as **pETH**; the curve captures volatility.
2. **Collateral.** pETH is deposited as the sole collateral for **CDPs**.
3. **Borrow.** A CDP mints **pUSD** (or another pAsset) against that pETH, for scalable onchain yield.
4. **Stewardship.** Burning pETH in a conversion auction mints **POLAR**, raising the pETH floor price and releasing ETH.

This page is the system map. Each term is defined briefly and links to its full treatment.

## The assets

A synthetic debt asset minted from a Polaris CDP (pUSD, pGOLD, pCHF, …). Each is overcollateralized by pETH and counterparty-free. See [The pAsset Catalog](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog).

The flagship pAsset, pegged to the US dollar, backed by pETH collateral. See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).

ETH deposited into a two-way [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth). It is not CDP-minted debt; it is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts, has a contract-defined floor, and is yield-bearing.

The protocol's stewardship token. After a fixed distribution at launch, new POLAR can be minted *only* by burning pETH in a [conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions). **vePOLAR**, a vote-escrow layer for stewardship rights, is forthcoming.

## Minting & positions

Collateralized Debt Position: the mechanism behind minting. You post collateral and mint the pAsset directly from the protocol, not by borrowing another user's funds. See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).

Your individual borrowing position in a Polaris CDP. Each trove is isolated, with its own collateral, debt, and loan-to-value (LTV). See [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove).

Polaris is a *factory* of CDP instances, each issuing a different pAsset but all sharing the same pETH collateral and bonding curve.

## Keeping the peg

Exchanging a pAsset for its underlying collateral at face value (minus a fee) when it trades *below* peg. Restores the floor. See [Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations).

The inverse of redemption. Arbitrageurs add pETH to troves to mint pAssets when the pAsset trades *above* peg. Restores the ceiling.

Redemption and minting volume serve as onchain proxy signals for the peg, letting Polaris set rates without an external oracle.

## Risk states

If a trove's LTV exceeds the maximum, it is liquidated, and the [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield) absorbs the debt — or, if the pool is too small, the debt and collateral redistribute to other troves. See [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).

A protocol-wide protective state entered when the system-wide reserve-backing-to-debt ratio (the [TCR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources)) falls below a fixed threshold. See [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode).

The mathematically enforced minimum price of pETH (in ETH), and that floor as a percentage of market price (your maximum drawdown). See [The Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price).

## Yield & coordination

A reserve of pAssets that processes liquidations and is the primary venue for earning yield. See [The Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield).

The mechanism that mints POLAR by burning pETH. See [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions).

Receives pETH from conversions and bonding-curve swap fees, then buffers and distributes it as yield to troves, pro-rata to their debt. See [The Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router).

A stewardship role steers *quantitative parameters*, with safety-critical bounds hardcoded in the contracts and the remaining bounds set at audited deployment. It does not govern the immutable core. See [Stewardship, not Governance](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship).

Next steps:
- [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status): Official app and network status, plus the shortest path from "I have ETH" to using Polaris.
- [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting): How the CDP works from first principles.
- [Why Polaris?](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/why-polaris): The stablecoin trilemma and how Polaris resolves it.

---

## Minting Polaris pAssets

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting.md

## The CDP model

Polaris issues pAssets through a **Collateralized Debt Position (CDP)**. From the outside it looks like borrowing against collateral, but underneath it differs from a money market like Aave.

- A **money market** matches lenders and borrowers through a shared pool. You borrow funds another user supplied; an interest rate floats with pool utilization.
- A **CDP** lets each borrower **mint** the pAsset directly from the protocol. You aren't borrowing anyone's deposit; you're your own mini central bank: post collateral ⇒ mint a pAsset ⇒ owe that debt back.

Each position is isolated in a **[trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove)** with its own collateral, debt, and loan-to-value (LTV). Users of a given pAsset share only (1) the pAsset and its peg state, and (2) system-wide parameters like the [Total Collateralization Ratio and Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode). That isolation bounds the blast radius: each pAsset keeps its own debt accounting, so trouble in one cannot reach the troves or collateral of another.

## Borrower terminology

Term you may see | Docs term | Meaning

**Borrow** | User action | The app tab/action for taking on debt.
**CDP** | Mechanism | The collateralized debt contract model for a pAsset instance.
**Trove** | Position | Your individual pAsset borrowing position: collateral, debt, and LTV.
**Loan / Reserve Loan** | Separate product | A WETH loan against fpETH floor-value collateral, not a pAsset trove.

When these docs say **trove**, they mean a pAsset debt position. When the app says **Reserve Loan**, read [pETH Split: fpETH and vpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) instead; reserve loans use fpETH, not pETH trove collateral.

### Strengths and trade-offs

The CDP model is ~8 years proven, its strengths and weaknesses well understood; Polaris's design amplifies the former and dampens the latter.

Strengths | Trade-offs

**Trustless** — logic fully enforced onchain, no intermediaries | **Liquidation risk** — collateral below threshold can be [liquidated](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations)
**Transparent** — ratios and liquidation logic auditable onchain | **Volatility** — sharp collateral drops can trigger liquidations
**Optionality** — unlock liquidity without selling your assets | **Smart-contract risk** — mitigated by audits, never zero
**Debt-asset agnostic** — the minted asset can be anything | **Capital inefficiency** — overcollateralization leaves capital idle

## What Polaris does differently

### Collateral is pETH, and only pETH

Polaris does not accept wstETH or other staked-ETH tokens: they would be external dependencies that cap scaling. It accepts only **[pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth)**, ETH deposited into the bonding curve. This dampens the CDP model's capital-inefficiency weakness, because pETH is:

1. an ETH proxy with a contract-defined floor, and
2. **yield-bearing** — collateral pETH also earns from other parts of the system (e.g. ETH released by conversions).

### Autonomous, market-aware interest rates

Setting CDP interest rates is where most designs struggle: Maker still uses governance-set rates (spiky, unpredictable); Liquity v1 had none (sustained supply pressure as T-bill rates rose); Liquity v2 pushes rate-setting onto users.

Polaris sets rates **autonomously, without a direct market-price feed for the pAsset**, by reading signals already inside the system:

- **Redemptions are profitable when pUSD < \$1** ⇒ a spike in redemption volume signals a downward depeg ⇒ raise the rate.
- **Minting is profitable when pUSD > \$1** ⇒ a spike in minting volume signals an upward depeg ⇒ lower the rate.

This gives the protocol an internal peg-pressure signal, not a substitute for every oracle in the system. Collateral valuation, non-dollar pAsset reference units, stale-price checks, and liquidation math still depend on the final audited pricing paths for those assets. See [Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations) for the mechanism behind the peg-pressure signal.

## Ready to mint

Minting a pAsset means opening a trove. See **[Open a Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove)** for the steps, and **[The pAsset Catalog](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog)** for what you can mint.

---

[CDPs Mint Dollars. Polaris Mints Anything](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything/)
From Maker to Liquity, CDP models shaped DeFi lending. Polaris takes the blueprint further with pETH, self-adjusting rates, and a factory for stablecoins pegged to anything.

---

## Managing a Polaris Trove

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove.md

A trove is a living position. After you [open it](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove), you can adjust it freely: add collateral, mint more, repay debt, or close it. The app-facing health metric is **loan-to-value (LTV)**.

**Scope:** this page covers pAsset troves/CDPs (shown under **Borrow**), not reserve loans. For the full vocabulary, see [Borrower terminology](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting#borrower-terminology).

## Loan-to-value (LTV)

Your **loan-to-value (LTV)** is the recorded value of your debt divided by the value of your trove's collateral:

> LTV = debt face value ÷ collateral value

Collateral value comes from the protocol's collateral pricing path. Debt value is what you owe in that pAsset's reference unit, including accrued interest; it is not the temporary market price at which you might buy the pAsset on a DEX. A trove holding pETH worth \$15,000 against 10,000 pUSD of debt has an LTV of 66.7%. Lower is safer.

Some protocol materials and parameters use **ICR** or **MCR**, the inverse collateral ratio:

> ICR = collateral value ÷ debt face value

That means 150% ICR is the same position as 66.7% LTV. A maximum LTV is the inverse of a minimum collateral ratio. Two rules matter:

- Above the **maximum LTV**, the trove can be [liquidated](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).
- [Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations) draw collateral from every open trove collectively, pro-rata to its recorded collateral and regardless of LTV. Lower LTV protects against liquidation, not redemption.

Your LTV moves on its own as ETH's price moves — you need not touch the trove for it to drift. Managing the trove is mostly about keeping LTV below the risk line with enough room for volatility.

## Trove Health Simulator

The simulator below defaults to the current Public Testnet 1 MCR of 115%, equivalent to about 87.0% max LTV, not the Emergency Mode action MCR of 150%. Always verify the live value on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) and in the app before signing.

Minting more or withdrawing collateral raises your LTV. Cross the maximum and your trove is liquidated automatically: your collateral covers the debt plus a liquidation penalty, with no margin call, and only the surplus is returned for you to claim. Redemptions can also reach your position while it is still solvent — there is no LTV exemption — shrinking your collateral and pETH exposure proportionally without your consent. See the Risk Disclosure.

## Values to check

Current MCR, max LTV equivalents, Recovery Mode, fee, decay, minimum-debt, and dust values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). Before changing a trove, check the live app quote and the parameter page for the pAsset you are managing.

## Add collateral

Deposit more pETH into the trove to **lower your LTV** without changing your debt. This is the simplest way to step back from a liquidation threshold during a drawdown. (Need pETH? Mint it on the [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth).)

## Mint more / repay debt

- **Mint more.** If your LTV has room, mint more pAsset against the same collateral. This *raises* your LTV, so leave a buffer.
- **Repay debt.** Burn pAsset back into the trove to reduce your debt and *lower* your LTV. Repaying is the other lever for recovering a thinning position, and it frees headroom to withdraw collateral.

## Monitor interest accrual

Borrowers pay an [autonomous, market-aware interest rate](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting) that accrues continuously against your trove. Interest adds to your debt over time, so your LTV drifts *up* even if ETH holds flat and you do nothing. Check your accrued debt periodically and repay or top up so accrual does not quietly walk you toward a threshold.

Collateral pETH also *earns* yield while it sits in the trove, partly offsetting the interest cost; see [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting) for how the effective rate is composed.

### Worked example: interest accrual

Suppose your trove owes 10,000 pUSD and the app quotes an 8% annualized borrow rate. As a rough estimate, before rate changes and compounding:

Period | Interest estimate | New debt

30 days | 10,000 × 8% × 30 / 365 = 65.75 pUSD | 10,065.75 pUSD
90 days | 10,000 × 8% × 90 / 365 = 197.26 pUSD | 10,197.26 pUSD

If your collateral value stays flat at \$15,000, the 30-day estimate moves your LTV from 66.7% to about 67.1%. The contract may accrue continuously and the rate may change, so use the app's recorded debt for precise decisions.

## Close the trove

Closing is the reverse of opening (**repay debt ⇒ withdraw collateral**):

1. **Repay the full debt.** Burn enough pAsset to clear the recorded debt, including accrued interest. You must hold (or re-acquire) the pAsset.
2. **Withdraw collateral.** With the debt at zero, withdraw your pETH. The trove is closed.

If you do not hold enough pAsset to repay (for example, you sold it), buy it back on the market first.

## How LTV maps to your exposure

LTV | Liquidation exposure | Redemption exposure

Low | Minimal — survives large ETH drops | Low — redemptions reduce every trove pro-rata to its collateral, and a large buffer keeps your LTV healthy afterward
Moderate | Survives normal volatility | Moderate — a system-wide redemption shrinks your collateral proportionally and can erode your buffer
Near max | High — a sharp ETH move liquidates you | A system-wide redemption reduces your collateral pro-rata and, on thin margin, can leave you closer to the liquidation line

The takeaway: a buffer is not wasted capital, it is the price of staying in control of your position. Keep enough margin that ordinary volatility and quiet interest accrual cannot force an outcome you did not choose.

Next steps:
- [Open a Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove): Step-by-step guide to opening your first trove.
- [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations): Understand what happens if your LTV crosses the maximum.
- [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield): Earn yield by depositing pAssets.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Borrow, Manage Trove, borrow, mint, open trove, repay, close trove, pUSD, pGOLD, LTV, loan-to-value, official app, trove, manage trove, borrower trove.

---

## Open a Polaris Trove

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove.md

A **trove** is your individual borrowing position: its own collateral, debt, and loan-to-value (LTV). Opening one is how you mint a pAsset: **connect wallet ⇒ acquire pETH ⇒ deposit pETH as collateral ⇒ choose and mint a pAsset ⇒ confirm ⇒ monitor your LTV**. This page walks each step; for the concepts behind it, see [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting) and [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove).

App anchor: **Borrow** tab -> pAsset trove flow. If the app says CDP, it is referring to the same pAsset debt-position mechanism this page calls a trove.

For the full **Borrow / CDP / trove / reserve loan** vocabulary — including why a reserve loan is not a pAsset trove — see [Borrower terminology](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting#borrower-terminology).

## Before you start

- A wallet with the right asset for the environment: **Sepolia ETH** for Public Testnet 1 or **ETH on Ethereum mainnet** after production launch.
- A target pAsset in mind — see [the catalog](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog).

## Parameters

Current testnet parameters and production-finality status live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). Use the app quote and live contract state before signing.

## Steps

### Connect your wallet

Open the Polaris app and connect.

### Acquire pETH

Deposit ETH into the [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth) to receive pETH. pETH is the *only* collateral Polaris accepts. If you already hold pETH, skip this step.

### Deposit pETH as collateral

Choose how much pETH to lock in the trove. This collateral stays in the trove until you withdraw it.

### Choose pAsset and amount

Pick the pAsset (pUSD, pGOLD, …) and the amount of debt to take on. The app shows the resulting **loan-to-value (LTV)** — your debt value divided by your collateral value — before you confirm.

### Confirm

Sign the transaction. The minted pAsset arrives in your wallet; the debt is recorded against your trove.

### Monitor your LTV

Your LTV moves with the price of ETH and with any changes you make. Watch it from the trove dashboard and adjust before it nears the maximum. See [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove).

## Choose an LTV buffer

Your LTV is the number that determines liquidation risk. Keep enough room below the maximum to absorb ETH price moves, interest accrual, gas delays, and redemption impact.

Redemptions are different from liquidations: lower LTV does not exempt your trove from proportional redemptions, but it can keep the smaller post-redemption position away from the liquidation line. See [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) for buffer strategy and examples.

Next steps:
- [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove): Adjust, monitor, and close your trove safely.
- [The Bonding Curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth): Acquire pETH, the only collateral Polaris accepts.
- [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield): Earn yield by depositing the pAssets you mint.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Borrow, Manage Trove, borrow, mint, open trove, repay, close trove, pUSD, pGOLD, LTV, loan-to-value, official app, trove, manage trove, borrower trove.

---

## Polaris Asset Catalog

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog.md

A **pAsset** is a synthetic debt asset minted from a Polaris CDP against pETH collateral. **pETH is different:** it is the bonding-curve collateral token that backs pAssets, not debt minted from a trove.

> **Current Public Testnet 1 focus:** pUSD and pGOLD are Testnet-active on Sepolia. pCHF, pBigMac, and other catalog ideas are illustrative or future until [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) says otherwise.

## Polaris assets

Asset | Tracks | Current status | Notes

**pUSD** | US dollar | **Testnet-active** | The flagship pAsset. See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).
**pETH** | ETH | **Testnet-active collateral** | *Special.* Not minted from a trove: it is the collateral itself, produced by the [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth). It backs every other pAsset.
**pGOLD** | 1 ounce of gold | **Testnet-active** | Decentralized gold exposure, backed by pETH rather than bullion. Public Testnet 1 uses Sepolia testnet price-feed plumbing; see the comparison below.
**pCHF** | Swiss franc | **Illustrative / future** | A non-USD fiat peg, demonstrating that the model is debt-asset agnostic across currencies. Not Testnet-active unless Launch Status later lists it.
**pBigMac** | The [Big Mac Index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_Mac_Index) | **Illustrative / future** | An example of a non-financial reference asset. Not Testnet-active unless Launch Status later lists it.

## pGOLD vs centralized gold (XAUT/PAXG)

pGOLD and the incumbent gold tokens both give gold-price exposure, but they are structurally different products.

Property | pGOLD | XAUT / PAXG

Collateral | pETH, fully onchain | Physical gold in custody
Redemption | Permissionless, no protocol-level minimum published yet | Issuer-gated; account requirements and redemption minimums depend on the issuer's current terms
KYC | None | Required to redeem
Physical custody | None — nothing to store or insure | Vaulted bullion, with custody risk
Counterparty | Polaris protocol only | Tether / Paxos as issuer

Image: pGOLD versus centralized gold tokens (XAUT/PAXG): pGOLD is backed by onchain pETH with permissionless, KYC-free access and Polaris as the only counterparty, while XAUT/PAXG are backed by vaulted bullion with issuer-gated, KYC-required redemption and custody risk

pGOLD inherits ETH-collateral and smart-contract risk where XAUT/PAXG inherit custodial and issuer risk. What pGOLD removes is the permissioned issuer layer.

Image: Borrowing pGOLD is not shorting gold: deposit pETH and borrow pGOLD, then your strategy sets the exposure — hold or LP the gold pair for gold-neutral exposure, or sell pGOLD for pETH (or loop) to take short-gold exposure

*Minting pGOLD against pETH is a borrowing action; whether you end up long pETH, gold-neutral, or short gold depends on what you do with the pGOLD.*

## StablecoinOS: an open catalog

The CDP model is **debt-asset agnostic**: it can mint a synthetic pegged to any asset with robust reference pricing and risk parameters. Polaris uses **StablecoinOS** as a factory of CDP instances: one CDP per pAsset, all sharing the same pETH collateral pool and bonding curve.

Shared collateral lets teams add a new pAsset without bootstrapping a fresh collateral base. Each instance still has its own debt accounting, so stress in one pAsset does not directly liquidate another pAsset's troves.

Image: StablecoinOS as a factory: ETH enters one shared bonding curve to mint pETH, which collateralizes multiple independent CDP instances that each mint a different pAsset — pUSD, pGOLD, pCHF — over the same pETH collateral base

---

[pGOLD: Finishing What DigixDAO Started](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pGOLD-finishing-what-digixdao-started/)
How pGOLD breaks the XAUT/PAXG duopoly without touching a single gold bar.

---

## pETH Bonding Curve

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth.md

The bonding curve is the foundation of Polaris. From this one primitive come pETH, its rising floor, [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split), reserve loans, and value capture on every interaction.

## What is a bonding curve?

A two-way bonding curve lets you swap like a regular AMM pool, with one key difference: **it needs no liquidity providers.** Instead, it enforces a mathematical relationship between a token's price and its supply:

- Demand rises, tokens are minted, price increases.
- Tokens are burned/sold, price decreases.

Buying adds ETH to the reserve and **mints** pETH. Selling withdraws from the reserve and **burns** pETH. The result is contract-native liquidity with no external liquidity provider, subject to Ethereum liveness, available gas, MEV, slippage, and the bonding-curve contracts working as designed. You have used bonding curves without knowing it: every AMM is one (Uniswap, Curve), and Pump.fun is a well-known example.

Two things make pETH's curve different from Pump.fun's:

1. **Purpose**: Pump.fun raises capital to seed liquidity; Polaris uses the curve to *back* pETH permanently.
2. **Temporality**: Pump.fun's curve terminates at "graduation"; the pETH curve is designed to keep running.

## What's unique about pETH's curve

pETH's curve is **two-way** (ETH ⇒ pETH and pETH ⇒ ETH) and gives pETH a price floor measured in ETH under the curve assumptions. The full floor and floor-ratio explanation lives on [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price).

Image: pETH bonding curve: ETH deposits mint pETH along a power-law curve with a rising floor price

*Key takeaway: the curve is both the issuance path and the reserve-backed exit path; fees and conversion burns are designed to lift the floor.*

### The maths

The curve follows the formula:

- **α**: a scaling parameter of little importance.
- **Q**: the supply of pETH.
- **β**: the power-law exponent that sets the curve's behaviour.

As supply `Q` increases, the price of pETH in ETH increases. **β** sets how tightly pETH tracks ETH: a large β decouples pETH and makes it more volatile; a small β couples them closely. Current values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters).

The Public Testnet 1 beta is testnet-only. The production beta remains pending; verify [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) before using any numeric value.

## Interactive explorer

## pETH beyond the CDP

pETH is tailor-made collateral for Polaris CDPs, but it has a life of its own.

### Reserve loans and Split

You can [split pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) into **fpETH** (the floor leg) and **vpETH** (the premium leg). fpETH can collateralize **reserve loans** — WETH borrowed against floor-value, sized to the floor price so premium swings don't liquidate the position. See [pETH Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) for the full mechanism, risks, and merge path.

### DeFi composability

pETH's rising floor and eventual deep ETH backing could make it a prime asset *outside* Polaris too. A pETH/ETH market on Aave, Morpho, or Euler is a natural fit, much like stETH across DeFi today.

## pETH vs stETH

stETH is a prime asset, but it lacks contract-native liquidity from its own reserve. Native withdrawals depend on the validator exit queue and can vary; DEX liquidity is deep, but depends on liquidity providers who can stop supplying in a panic.

Feature | **pETH** | **stETH**

Liquidity | Bonding-curve reserve, subject to contract and chain liveness | Deep, but LP-dependent
Withdrawal | Onchain sell path, with gas/slippage/MEV | Validator-exit queue; timing varies
Price floor | Contract-enforced under audited invariants | None
Main risk | Price risk vs ETH, bounded by the floor under the curve assumptions | LP/liquidity risk

stETH is a major ETH asset, but it does not provide pETH's reserve-backed onchain sell path.

## Risk overview

pETH carries three main risks:

1. **Technical risk**: the bonding curve is a smart contract; technical risk is unavoidable, mitigated through audits and modeling.
2. **Execution risk**: using the curve still requires Ethereum liveness, available gas, and transactions that can tolerate slippage and MEV.
3. **Price risk**: pETH is volatile against ETH. Your exposure depends on your entry price relative to the floor, i.e. the [floor ratio](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price): a higher ratio means a smaller curve-implied drawdown before execution costs.

The curve design removes liquidity-provider withdrawal risk. That does not make execution riskless: the curve provides a contract-defined buy/sell path and floor while the relevant invariants hold.

Next steps:
- [The Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price): Understand the mathematically enforced minimum price of pETH.
- [Open a Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove): Use pETH as collateral to mint your first pAsset.
- [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts): The one-page map of the entire Polaris system.

---

[The Bonding Curve: Polaris' Secret Weapon](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/bonding-curve/)
How a single mechanism enables pETH's rising floor, contract-native liquidity, and protocol-wide value capture.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price, pETH bonding curve, bonding curve, pETH floor.

---

## pETH Floor Price

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price.md

## What it is

The **floor price** is pETH's mathematically enforced minimum price, in ETH, under the bonding-curve invariants. It is the curve price for the *last person out* if everyone exited the bonding curve at once — assuming the contracts work as designed, Ethereum is live, and users can pay execution costs. Because most exiters get a better curve price, it is a protocol worst case, not an average.

The floor is not a promise or a target defended by a treasury. It is a direct consequence of the bonding curve's reserves and supply, enforced by immutable code and dependent on the audited invariants holding.

## Why it is designed to rise

Two mechanisms push the floor up under the intended curve invariants. Normal protocol actions are not designed to push it down:

- **Swap fees feed the burn.** Every swap on the [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth) charges a pETH fee that accrues to the protocol's Fee Router. The Fee Router periodically floor-sells and burns accumulated pETH, reducing supply against an unchanged-or-growing ETH reserve and raising the floor.
- **Conversion auctions.** Minting POLAR requires [burning pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions), which again reduces supply and lifts the floor.

The result is a staircase under normal operation: pETH's market price fluctuates above the floor, while the floor itself steps upward over time.

Image: The rising pETH floor as a staircase stepping up over time, each step driven by a swap-fee burn or a conversion-auction burn that permanently reduces pETH supply

*Each swap-fee burn and conversion auction removes pETH supply, ratcheting the floor up a step — under the curve assumptions.*

## The floor ratio

The **floor ratio** expresses the floor as a percentage of the current market price:

$$
\text{Floor ratio} = \frac{\text{Floor price}}{\text{Market price}} \times 100
$$

It tells you the curve-implied drawdown to the floor before gas, slippage, MEV, and smart-contract or chain-liveness risk. A 90% floor ratio means 10% downside to the floor in ETH terms under those assumptions; 67% means 33% downside.

Floor ratio | Curve-implied drawdown to floor

90% | 10%
67% | 33%
55% | 45%

Image: The floor ratio: a volatile pETH market-price line above a steadily rising floor line; a worked example with market price 1.09 and floor 0.60 gives a 55% floor ratio and a 45% maximum drawdown to the floor

*A higher floor ratio means a smaller curve-implied drawdown to the floor — before gas, slippage, MEV, and technical risk.*

The floor ratio is itself volatile, driven by market sentiment and activity:

- In phases of **wild growth**, the market price runs far ahead of the floor, so the ratio can fall, sometimes below 50%.
- As the market price **normalizes**, the floor catches up and the ratio rises, until the limited remaining downside makes pETH attractive again.

## Assumptions behind the floor

Assumption | Why it matters

Bonding-curve contracts function as audited | The floor is a contract invariant, not an offchain guarantee
Ethereum remains live | Exiting requires a transaction that can be included
Users can pay gas | High gas can make small exits uneconomic
Slippage and MEV are tolerated or bounded | The quoted curve price and realized execution can differ
The relevant invariants stay unchanged and unbroken | The floor depends on reserves, supply accounting, and burn/mint rules

> Your risk in pETH is decided at entry: the spread between your entry price and the floor (the floor ratio), plus execution and technical risk. Under the intended curve invariant, normal protocol activity moves the floor up, not down.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price, pETH bonding curve, bonding curve, pETH floor.

---

## pETH Split: fpETH and vpETH

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split.md

The **Split** mechanism separates pETH into two matching tokens:

```
1 pETH -> 1 fpETH + 1 vpETH
```

Splitting does not create extra value. It separates the pETH position into a **floor leg** and a **premium leg** so users can hold, trade, merge, or borrow against the part of pETH they actually want.

## The two legs

Token | Meaning | Main use

**fpETH** | Floor-price pETH. It tracks the pETH floor-value claim under the splitter and bonding-curve assumptions. | Hold floor carry, redeem for WETH through the splitter, or use as collateral in reserve loans.
**vpETH** | Volatility-premium pETH. It represents exposure to pETH's market value above the floor. | Hold or trade the premium/upside leg after separating out the floor claim.

Think of pETH as:

```
pETH value = floor value + premium above floor
```

fpETH isolates the floor-value side. vpETH isolates the remaining premium side. If pETH trades close to its floor, vpETH can become worth very little. If pETH trades far above its floor, vpETH carries most of that premium exposure.

## Split and merge

**Split** deposits pETH into the splitter contract and mints equal amounts of fpETH and vpETH to your wallet.

**Merge** burns equal amounts of fpETH and vpETH and releases the matching pETH from the splitter.

You need both legs in equal amounts to merge back into pETH. If you sold or deposited one leg, you must reacquire it before merging.

## fpETH redemption

fpETH can be redeemed through the splitter for WETH at the current pETH floor-price value under the contract assumptions. That redemption is the reason fpETH is the **floor leg**.

This is not an offchain guarantee. It depends on the splitter, bonding curve, reserve accounting, Ethereum liveness, gas availability, and the relevant invariants holding.

## Reserve loans

Reserve loans use **fpETH** as collateral, and each loan is recorded onchain as an NFT. The borrowable WETH amount is based on the fpETH amount and current pETH floor price, not on pETH's market premium.

That design reduces price-liquidation risk from pETH premium swings, but it does not make the position risk-free:

- Debt can accrue according to the reserve-loan fee model.
- Closing or adjusting can be affected by floor-price changes before inclusion.
- You still need WETH to repay and unlock fpETH.
- Smart-contract, approval, oracle, gas, and chain-liveness risks remain.

Reserve loans are separate from pAsset troves. A trove mints pUSD, pGOLD, or another pAsset against pETH collateral. A reserve loan borrows WETH against fpETH.

## Floor carry

**Floor carry** is the return a fpETH holder may realize as the pETH floor rises. If the floor price increases while you hold fpETH, the floor-value redemption claim increases under the contract assumptions.

Floor carry is not a fixed APR. It depends on actual floor growth from protocol activity, execution costs, market pricing of fpETH, and whether the redemption path is available when you use it.

## Related app tabs

- [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split) for splitting and merging.
- [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow) for reserve loans.
- [Dashboard](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app) for live token prices; the floor and market price show on the Split tab.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Swap, swap, Split, fpETH, vpETH, pETH, floor price, pETH bonding curve, bonding curve, pETH floor.

---

## POLAR Token, Staking, and Conversion

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar.md

POLAR is Polaris's stewardship token and outward-facing growth lever. After the initial distribution, new POLAR is intended to enter through one path: [burn pETH in a conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions).

App anchors: **Earn** for current POLAR Staking where exposed, and **Advanced** or a conversion panel for pETH -> POLAR conversion actions.

> **Status distinction:** The app may expose **POLAR Staking** as a current stake/unstake/claim surface on testnet. **vePOLAR locking** is separate and forthcoming: lock durations, vote-power decay, voting rules, and production addresses are pending. Do not use a conversion, staking, or locking interface unless [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) and [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#contracts-and-addresses) point to it.

## Why a third token

Polaris runs on three engines: pETH, the pAssets, and POLAR. The first two handle everything the protocol *needs* to function: pETH backs the CDPs, the Stability Pool generates yield, interest rates self-adjust, and liquidations process autonomously. **Polaris works without POLAR.**

So why add a third token? Because functioning and scaling differ. The design splits along an **inward- vs. outward-facing** logic:

Function | Handled by | Examples

**Inward-facing** — sustain the core | pETH + pAssets | interest rates, floor growth, Stability Pool incentivization, liquidations
**Outward-facing** — grow the ecosystem | POLAR | external DEX liquidity, Flow onboarding, [StablecoinOS](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts) coordination

POLAR is deliberately separated from the core so the core's sustainability never depends on it. If POLAR incentives dried up tomorrow, pUSD would still mint, the bonding curve would still function, and the Stability Pool would still pay yield. The base protocol does not need POLAR to survive, but it scales far better with it.

## Minted only by burning pETH

There is no planned team mint function or calendar inflation schedule disconnected from protocol activity. The detailed economics live on [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics); the auction mechanics live on [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions).

## POLAR Staking and future vePOLAR

POLAR can be put to work two ways: current **POLAR Staking** where exposed, and forthcoming **[vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar)** locking for stewardship rights. See [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics#polar-staking-vs-vepolar) for the full distinction.

## Go deeper

Page | What it answers

[POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics) | Issuance structure, current staking, future vePOLAR, and what is still pending.
[POLAR Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) | How spike-and-decay pricing, pETH burns, rebates, and floor growth work, plus how to convert.
[vePOLAR Locking and Stewardship](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar) | The forthcoming vote-escrow layer and its stewardship role.

POLAR is not a claim on a treasury or a vote in an unbounded DAO. It is the token used for outward-facing growth and bounded stewardship.

Next steps:
- [POLAR Staking](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn): Current stake, unstake, and claim surface where exposed.
- [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions): How spike-and-decay pricing, rebates, and floor growth work.
- [Stewardship](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship): Bounded parameter control and future vePOLAR.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: POLAR, convert, lock, vePOLAR, burn pETH, conversion auction, POLAR token, POLAR staking, POLAR tokenomics.

---

## POLAR Conversion Auctions

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions.md

Conversion auctions let converters **burn pETH to mint new POLAR**. This page covers the auction mechanics; POLAR's broader economics live on [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics).

Image: The conversion mechanism: a converter burns pETH, which mints new POLAR, lifts the pETH floor price, and releases ETH from the bonding-curve reserve; the released ETH splits into a converter rebate and pETH forwarded to the Fee Router

## Spike-and-decay pricing
