Conversion runs as a **continuous, permissionless auction**. Anyone can convert pETH to POLAR at any moment; whether it is *profitable* depends on the conversion price versus POLAR's market price. The price follows a **spike-and-decay** model:

1. The conversion price **continuously decays**. The longer nobody converts, the cheaper the offered price — and the more attractive conversion gets.
2. When a conversion happens, the price **spikes upward**, proportional to the fraction of POLAR supply just inflated. Minting 1% of supply spikes the price far more than minting 0.01%.
3. After the spike, decay resumes until the next conversion.

The result is a natural rhythm: the price decays until an arbitrageur sees it drift below POLAR's market price, converts to capture the spread, and spikes it back up — closing the opportunity until decay reopens it.

Image: Spike-and-decay conversion pricing: the conversion price decays continuously until it drops below POLAR's market price, opening an arbitrage window; each conversion spikes the price up by an amount proportional to the fraction of POLAR supply minted, then decay resumes

> **Note:**
> The spike is computed against **POLAR supply, not pETH supply**. This steers conversions toward small, frequent operations: a whale converting a large amount in one go faces rapidly diminishing returns as each increment spikes the price against them. The mechanism self-regulates toward steady, incremental minting.

In practice, conversions will mostly be executed by arbitrage bots and MEV searchers. The dynamics are tuned for fast, competitive execution.

> *Exact decay and spike parameters are finalized closer to launch, set through stewardship. The converter rebate is bounded between 0% and 100%; final decay and spike bounds are pending.*

## What burning pETH does

Burning pETH through a conversion has two effects beyond minting POLAR:

- **ETH is released from the bonding curve reserve.** The ETH backing the burned pETH is freed as conversion gain.
- **The [floor price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) can rise under the curve assumptions.** Reducing supply while preserving reserve backing lifts the modeled floor. Conversions are one-way: POLAR cannot be converted back to pETH.

Image: The floor-price ratchet: burning pETH through conversions contracts supply along the power-law curve, lifting the floor from one level to a higher one; because conversions are one-way, the floor steps up and does not come back down

See [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) for the full floor and floor-ratio explanation.

## The converter rebate

Before released ETH becomes system yield, a slice can return to the converter. The **converter rebate** is a stewardship-controlled dial between conversion volume and system yield:

Rebate | Effect

**0%** | Maximum yield to the system; conversions cost the most. Sensible when POLAR's market cap exceeds pETH's.
**100%** | Zero system yield; conversions are cheapest (effective cost set by the [floor ratio](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price)). An emergency lever to drive volume when the floor is stalling.
**In between** | The usual operating range, shifting with market conditions.

Stewards watch the **POLAR / pETH market-cap ratio**: when POLAR's is larger, a lower rebate is easier to defend; when it flips, higher rebates may support conversion volume.

> **Note:**
> The total ETH released from the curve is **unaffected** by the rebate in the model. The floor-price impact is the same however the ETH is split afterward, assuming the contracts and curve math execute as designed. The rebate reshapes the *distribution* of value, not the underlying collateral dynamics.

After the rebate, the remaining ETH is swapped into pETH and forwarded to the **[Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router)**, which buffers it and distributes the yield to pETH troves, pro-rata to their recorded debt.

## What conversion does *not* do

- **It does not manufacture demand for POLAR.** If POLAR trades below the conversion price, no conversions happen until decay catches up.
- **Parameters carry weight.** Decay too fast can cause frequent dilution; decay too slow can stall conversion activity. Final guardrails remain pending.

## How to convert

Conversions are a continuous, permissionless auction tuned for arbitrage bots and MEV searchers, so converting profitably means competing with them. Most users will hold POLAR, use current POLAR Staking where the app exposes it, or wait for [vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar) locking instead of converting directly.

**You need** pETH to burn (bought on the [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth) or a DEX) and a wallet connected to the Polaris app. On Public Testnet 1 this is Sepolia-only; production conversions are not actionable until [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the official app, conversion contract, final parameters, and audit status.

### Open the conversion panel

It shows the live **conversion price** and POLAR's **market price** side by side.

### Watch the decaying price

The conversion price decays continuously and spikes upward each time someone converts. A conversion is profitable when it drifts **below** POLAR's market price; that spread is your margin.

### Read the quote

Enter a pETH amount. The panel previews the **POLAR you will receive**, the **ETH rebate** at the current [rebate](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions#the-converter-rebate) setting, and the resulting **price spike**. Because the spike scales with the fraction of POLAR supply minted, larger amounts price progressively worse; favor smaller increments.

### Decide

Convert only while the previewed POLAR (plus rebate) beats acquiring POLAR on the open market. If the spread has closed, wait for decay to reopen it.

### Burn pETH ⇒ receive POLAR

Confirm the transaction. Your pETH is **permanently burned**, you receive newly minted POLAR, and any ETH rebate goes to your wallet.

### Stake, future-lock, or hold

Stake POLAR where the current app exposes POLAR Staking, wait for [vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar) locking once audited deployment publishes it, or hold it.

[Burn pETH, Mint POLAR: The Conversion Mechanism](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/burn-pETH-mint-POLAR/)
How a single primitive connects Polaris' three engines, and why every conversion strengthens the protocol.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: POLAR, convert, lock, vePOLAR, burn pETH, conversion auction.

---

## POLAR Tokenomics

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics.md

This page consolidates POLAR's economics. Each piece has its own page; here they sit side by side. For the reasoning behind the token, read [The POLAR Token](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar); for the issuance mechanism, [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions).

> **Current status:** POLAR's total supply, distribution schedule, and vesting terms are pending and will be published at launch. This page describes the *structure* of POLAR's economics, fixed by design, not the final numbers, which are forthcoming. Treat any figure not stated here as not yet finalized.

## Issuance: minted only by burning pETH

After the initial distribution, new POLAR is intended to be mintable only by burning pETH in a [conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions). There is no planned team mint function or calendar-based inflation schedule.

- Every POLAR that enters circulation after launch is intended to require someone to burn pETH.
- Conversion runs as a continuous, permissionless [spike-and-decay auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions), so issuance tracks real arbitrage activity, not a fixed emission curve. Most conversions are executed by arbitrage bots and MEV searchers.

Each conversion burns pETH, releases ETH from the bonding-curve reserve as yield, and mints POLAR. See [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) for the floor mechanics.

## A bounded, productive dilution

Every conversion dilutes existing POLAR holders, but the dilution is bounded and buys a concrete improvement:

- **Bounded.** [Spike-and-decay pricing](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) makes large or rapid minting throttle itself, so issuance tracks arbitrage demand. Final parameter bounds are pending.
- **Productive.** Each unit of dilution corresponds to burned pETH and released ETH yield, unlike emissions disconnected from protocol activity.
- **Progressive for the team.** The founding team's outsized early share is diluted by the same mechanism: the faster Polaris grows, the more attractive conversions become, and the more that initial share shrinks. The dilution is enforced by code, not pledged in a forum.

## POLAR Staking vs vePOLAR

The app vocabulary matters:

- **POLAR Staking** is the current staking surface where a deployment exposes stake, unstake, and claim actions for routed rewards.
- **[vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar)** is the forthcoming vote-escrow layer. Locking POLAR for a period is intended to return vePOLAR, which grants stewardship rights and a yield path.

vePOLAR lock durations, vote-power decay, unlock rules, proposal rules, and the final yield split are pending. Do not treat current POLAR Staking as a substitute for vePOLAR voting rights.

## Where the yield comes from

POLAR's value accrual runs on hard-asset protocol revenue, buffered and routed by the **[Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router)** (pETH) and, separately, by the protocol's interest router (pAsset). It reaches three destinations:

Destination | What they receive

**Troves** | pETH yield from the Fee Router, credited pro-rata to recorded debt
**POLAR stakers** | A stewarded share of protocol interest, in pAsset terms where the staking surface is live
**vePOLAR lockers** | Forthcoming stewardship and yield path after audited locking deployment
**Flow recipients** | Revenue-sharing for whitelisted contracts via [Flows](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router) (forthcoming)

Inputs are protocol revenue, not emitted POLAR. The Fee Router handles pETH rewards; the interest router handles pAsset-denominated splits. Exact destination splits, production staking terms, and vePOLAR locking terms are pending.

## What POLAR does *not* have

The structure deliberately omits the usual extraction surfaces:

- **No gauges.** Stewardship cannot create POLAR emissions gauges.
- **No bribes.** No vote-buying markets are routed through the protocol.
- **No emissions.** Ecosystem incentives flow through [Flows](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router) as hard-asset revenue sharing, funded by protocol revenue, not token emissions.
- **No planned mint outside conversion.** Final deployment is intended to mint POLAR only through pETH conversion.

## The Polar Council

The **Polar Council** is a planned treasury-funded program for onboarding and ecosystem contributors, comparable in spirit to veAERO's Flight School. Initially team-managed; its allocation, operating framework, eligibility, and reporting requirements are pending publication.

---

**[Go deeper →](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/)**

Relevant app/search vocabulary: POLAR, convert, lock, vePOLAR, burn pETH, conversion auction, POLAR token, POLAR staking, POLAR tokenomics.

---

## pAsset Redemptions

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations.md

> **Actionability:** Redemptions are testnet-actionable only on Sepolia using test assets and current manifests. Production redemptions are not mainnet-actionable until [Polaris Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) publishes the official app, production addresses, final fee formula, dust rules, and audit status.

## What a redemption is

A **redemption** exchanges a pAsset for the **[pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth)** collateral backing it at the pAsset's protocol reference value, minus a fee. It pays when the pAsset trades *below* peg by more than the fee, gas, slippage, and execution risk.

If pUSD slips to \$0.98, a redeemer can buy pUSD cheaply, redeem it for about \$1 of pETH minus the fee, and sell or hold the pETH. The pETH comes from the collateral of troves carrying that pUSD debt, and their debt shrinks by the pUSD redeemed. No counterparty agrees to the trade; the protocol enforces it.

This puts an arbitrage floor under the pAsset's market price.

## Parameters

Redemption fees, decay factors, dust rules, and current network values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). This page explains the mechanism; the parameter page owns the numbers.

## How it restores the peg

A below-peg pAsset is a standing arbitrage. Walk the loop:

1. **Buy** pUSD on the market at \$0.98.
2. **Redeem** it for \$1 − fee of pETH.
3. **Sell** the pETH, pocket the spread.
4. Repeat until the arb closes.

Each pass buys pUSD on the open market, applying **upward price pressure**. Arbitrageurs keep going until pUSD is back at peg and the trade stops paying. Self-interest restores the peg, not intervention.

> **Note:** Redemptions handle the *below*-peg case. The *above*-peg case is handled by the inverse mechanism, **minting**, where arbitrageurs add pETH to troves to mint pAssets when the pAsset trades over peg. See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).

The two mechanisms are mirror images:

Mechanism | Direction | Arbitrageur does | Effect on troves

**Redemption** | pAsset < peg | Burns pAsset (repays debt) ⇒ takes pETH | Debt and collateral both shrink
**Minting** | pAsset > peg | Adds pETH ⇒ mints pAsset | Debt and collateral both grow

Their volumes also serve as the protocol's peg signal: a spike in redemptions reads as a downward depeg, a spike in mintings as an upward one. That is how Polaris sets [autonomous interest rates](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting) without a direct pUSD market-price feed.

Image: Interest rate and peg stability: above peg, minting volume rises and the interest rate falls to expand supply; below peg, redemption volume rises and the interest rate climbs to contract supply — each loop pushing the price back toward peg

*Redemption and minting volume are the onchain peg signal; the loops above show the mechanism's direction, not exact arithmetic.*

## Mechanics in detail

### Fees and fill limits

Current fee floors, decay factors, and fill constraints live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). The user-facing rule is simple: the redeemer receives the redeemed amount's reference value in pETH **minus** the fee and execution costs. If the fee, gas, pETH slippage, MEV risk, or price movement exceed the below-peg spread, the redemption is unprofitable.

The app should show a pre-confirmation quote once the interface is live. Treat examples here as arithmetic only.

### Borrower impact

A redemption targets the whole pAsset system, not a specific trove. It reduces debt and pETH collateral across **all** open troves for that pAsset, pro-rata to each trove's recorded collateral. There is no LTV queue, no "riskiest trove first," and no health metric that exempts a trove.

Being redeemed against is not a liquidation penalty: your debt is repaid at face value with your own collateral, so net equity is broadly unchanged before fees and rounding. The cost is control. Your trove becomes smaller, your pETH exposure falls, and future pETH-linked yield potential falls with it.

Lower LTV protects against **liquidation**, not redemption. It can still matter after a redemption, because the remaining LTV often improves but the position is smaller.

### MEV and transaction ordering

Redemptions are visible onchain transactions. Searchers can compete for the same spread, the pETH exit price can move, and system totals can shift before your transaction lands. Use the app's slippage limits, fee quotes, and any private-transaction support.

## Worked example: redemption impact

Assume pUSD trades at \$0.98, the redemption fee quote is 0.5%, and gas/slippage are ignored. A redeemer buys 10,000 pUSD for \$9,800 and redeems it for \$10,000 of pETH minus the 0.5% fee, or about \$9,950 of pETH. The gross spread is about \$150 before gas, slippage, MEV, and price movement.

Now the borrower side. A 4,000 pUSD redemption reduces total system debt by 4,000 pUSD and removes roughly \$4,000 of pETH from the pool. Both reductions are shared across every open trove in proportion to recorded collateral.

For intuition, here is the proportional share for one trove holding a fifth of the system's collateral, so it absorbs a fifth of the redemption (800 pUSD of debt and about \$800 of pETH):

Step | Collateral value | Debt | LTV

Before redemption | \$18,000 | 12,000 pUSD | 66.7%
After absorbing its pro-rata share | About \$17,200 | 11,200 pUSD | About 65.1%

The borrower did not repay voluntarily; the market did it through the redemption path. The trove is slightly safer by LTV, but smaller and less exposed to pETH.

## Before you act

1. Track your **LTV** with enough buffer for price moves, interest accrual, and redemption impact.
2. Treat a below-peg pAsset as a live signal that redemptions may shrink your position.
3. Use [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) for borrower levers and [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) for the full risk model.

---

## Next actions

- [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) for borrower levers.
- [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) for the forced-close path.
- [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) for current redemption values.

[CDPs Mint Dollars. Polaris Mints Anything](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything/)
From Maker to Liquity, CDP models shaped DeFi lending. Polaris takes the blueprint further with pETH, self-adjusting rates, and a factory for stablecoins pegged to anything.

---

## Polaris Liquidations

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations.md

## What a liquidation is

A **liquidation** closes a trove whose **LTV exceeds the maximum** — the point at which its collateral no longer comfortably covers its debt. The protocol cancels the trove's debt and seizes its pETH collateral.

The purpose is solvency. Every pAsset is a claim on collateral; leaving undercollateralized troves open would leave the pAsset not fully backed. Liquidations are how Polaris is designed to keep backing intact, subject to functioning contracts, live Ethereum execution, available gas, valid pricing data, keeper/executor availability, and audited invariants.

Unlike a [redemption](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations), a liquidation is not an arbitrage you can opt into at par — it is a forced close triggered by your own LTV crossing a hard line.

## Where the debt and collateral go

Liquidation runs in two tiers.

1. **The Stability Pool absorbs it first.** The [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield) holds a reserve of the pAsset. When a trove is liquidated, the pool burns pAsset equal to the trove's debt and receives its pETH collateral in return. Because liquidated troves are typically seized while still *overcollateralized* but past the LTV liquidation line, the pool usually receives more value in pETH than the pAsset it burns. That surplus is the depositors' compensation.

2. **Redistribution is the fallback.** If the Stability Pool is empty or too small to cover the debt, the trove's debt *and* collateral spread across the remaining troves on that pAsset, in proportion to their size. Other borrowers take on a slice of debt and a matching slice of pETH. The system stays solvent either way.

Path | Trigger | Absorbs debt | Receives collateral

**Stability Pool** | Pool has sufficient pAsset | Pool burns its pAsset | Depositors, pro-rata
**Redistribution** | Pool empty / insufficient | Other troves take it on | Other troves, pro-rata

**Collateral surplus.** When the Stability Pool absorbs a liquidation, the trove surrenders only what covers its debt, a liquidation penalty, and gas compensation for the liquidator. Any collateral beyond that goes to the **Collateral Surplus Pool**, where the former borrower can claim it. (Collateral spread through the redistribution fallback is not returned this way.)

## Values to check

Current MCR, Recovery Mode, fee, dust, and liquidation values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). This page explains the closeout path; the parameter page owns the numbers.

Before acting, check:

- The pAsset's max LTV or inverse minimum collateral ratio.
- Any liquidation penalty, gas compensation, and keeper incentive.
- Stability Pool coverage and whether redistribution can apply.
- Oracle freshness and fallback behavior.

## Worked example: liquidation

The MCR number below is the current Public Testnet 1 pUSD/pGOLD value as of June 8, 2026. Parameters publish this as a 115% minimum collateral ratio, equivalent to about 87.0% max LTV. A borrower has 10,000 pUSD of debt and pETH collateral valued at \$11,300, so the LTV is about 88.5% — above the current testnet maximum, and liquidatable.

If the Stability Pool is large enough, it burns 10,000 pUSD and receives the trove's pETH. The borrower's debt is cleared; collateral covers the debt plus the contract-defined liquidation penalty and gas compensation where available, with any surplus returned for the borrower to claim. Depositors receive pETH pro rata; in a normal overcollateralized liquidation, that pETH should be worth more than the pUSD burned, but the exact gain depends on the penalty, execution timing, pETH price, gas, and slippage.

If the Stability Pool is empty or too small, the uncovered portion follows the redistribution path: remaining troves inherit a proportional slice of both the liquidated debt and collateral.

## Risks

Liquidation is involuntary and can happen fast. A liquidated trove gives up its collateral to cover the debt plus a liquidation penalty and gas compensation; any surplus is returned to you to claim, but the penalty makes liquidation a real loss to avoid. Keep a **buffer** below max LTV so a sharp move in collateral price does not cross the line. The thinner your margin, the less room you have when volatility hits. See Managing Your Trove.

You are the system's first-loss backstop. In a flash crash, troves can be liquidated near — or in extreme cases below — 100% collateralization, and you may receive pETH worth less than the pAsset burned. Over a full cycle the surplus from healthy liquidations compensates for that risk, but it is not riskless yield. See The Stability Pool and the Risk Disclosure.

## How liquidation interacts with system health

A trove's LTV is not all that matters. When system-wide backing runs thin, the protocol enters [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode), which tightens risk-increasing borrower operations while it lasts. Liquidation eligibility is unchanged: a trove is liquidated whenever its LTV exceeds the maximum, in every mode. Recovery Mode is the system-level safeguard complementing the trove-level liquidation described here.

Next steps:
- [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove): Keep your LTV healthy and avoid liquidation.
- [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield): Understand how the pool absorbs liquidations.
- [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode): The system-level safeguard for thin backing.

---

[CDPs Mint Dollars. Polaris Mints Anything](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-mints-anything/)
From Maker to Liquity, CDP models shaped DeFi lending. Polaris takes the blueprint further with pETH, self-adjusting rates, and a factory for stablecoins pegged to anything.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: liquidation, liquidations, liquidate, liquidated, liquidation penalty.

---

## Polaris Recovery Mode

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode.md

## The Total Collateralization Ratio

The **Total Collateralization Ratio (TCR)** is the system-wide health metric for a pAsset. It is not a simple market-value-of-collateral over debt figure: it measures the bonding-curve reserve backing all the pETH collateral — the collateral scaled by the curve's reserve-backing fraction and valued at the reserve (redemption) price — divided by total pAsset debt outstanding.

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{\text{Reserve backing the collateral} \times \text{reserve price}}{\text{Total debt}}
$$

A high TCR means a deep, well-backed system; a low TCR means the reserve behind that pAsset is thin relative to its debt. In the contracts this is the reserve-to-debt ratio, and crossing the threshold triggers the protective state (internally called Emergency Mode).

## What Recovery Mode is

**Recovery Mode** is a protective state entered automatically when TCR drops below a set threshold. It is not a freeze or shutdown; it tightens borrower actions that would make system backing thinner.

## Parameters

Recovery Mode thresholds and current per-pAsset values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters). This page explains behavior, not current numbers.

## How behavior changes

Recovery Mode does **not** change which troves are liquidatable. In every mode, a trove is liquidatable only once its LTV exceeds the normal maximum. What tightens is the set of borrower actions allowed while the state is active.

Your action | Normal Mode | Recovery Mode

**Add collateral / repay debt** | Allowed | Allowed
**Withdraw collateral / mint more debt** | Allowed if LTV stays below the maximum | Allowed only if the resulting LTV stays below the stricter recovery maximum and does not increase your LTV
**Liquidation eligibility** | LTV above the maximum | LTV above the maximum (unchanged)

The principle: actions that strengthen backing stay open; actions that thin backing face a higher bar. Current trigger and action values are published on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters).

## Worked example: Recovery Mode

The trigger is the system's reserve-backing-to-debt ratio, so plain collateral value divided by debt is only a rough proxy. Treat the example as shape, not exact arithmetic.

Suppose the protective state is active for pUSD. A trove below the normal max LTV is **not** liquidated by Recovery Mode, but it may lose freedom to withdraw collateral or mint more debt. Those actions must clear the stricter recovery max LTV and cannot raise the trove's LTV. Adding collateral and repaying debt are always allowed.

The borrower's defense is the same as always: add collateral, repay debt, or both. For example, a trove with \$13,000 of collateral and 10,000 pUSD of debt is at 76.9% LTV; repaying 2,000 pUSD moves it to 8,000 / \$13,000, or 61.5% LTV, before fees and price movement — and lower LTV also widens your room to act while the system is stressed.

## How to make your trove safe

If the system is in or near Recovery Mode, the move is the same one that always protects a trove: lower your **LTV**.

1. **Add pETH collateral.** More collateral against the same debt lowers your LTV directly.
2. **Repay debt.** Burning pAsset to reduce what you owe lowers your LTV from the other side.
3. **Do both early.** Build the buffer before stress arrives, not while actions are already restricted.

See [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) for the mechanics, and [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) for what happens to a trove that exceeds the max LTV.

> **Warning:** Recovery Mode is most likely during volatile conditions when adding collateral is hardest. The reliable defense is a buffer maintained in calm markets. See the [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

---

## Polaris Stewardship, not Governance

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship.md

Governance risk is structural: unlimited scope, undefined boundaries, and misaligned incentives create capture risk.

Polaris's answer is neither a fully immutable protocol with no adjustable parameters nor an unbounded DAO. It is a third path: an immutable core plus a clearly scoped layer where human judgment is necessary. We call it **stewardship** rather than governance because the difference is structural, not cosmetic.

> **Current status:** Stewardship is not live yet. POLAR and vePOLAR contract addresses, lock terms, proposal thresholds, voting periods, execution delays, and final numeric parameter bounds await audited deployment. These docs describe the intended scope and lifecycle without inventing launch parameters.

## The core is immutable

The mechanisms that define Polaris **cannot be changed by anyone**: not the team, not vePOLAR, not a vote. They are enforced by immutable contracts:

- The [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth) backing pETH
- CDP logic and [trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) accounting
- [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations)
- [Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations)
- The [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield)

The base protocol does not need to be relearned after each vote, because the core logic is out of reach by design.

## What stewardship controls

Stewardship operates at the edges: StablecoinOS onboarding, permitted parameter dials, the POLAR stewardship layer, fee and interest splits, Flow participation, and treasury programs. Safety-critical adjustable values ship with hardcoded limits; the remaining numeric bounds are set at audited deployment.

Domain | Power | Constraint

**pAsset licensing** | Approve a team or instance to deploy a new pAsset on Polaris infrastructure | Standardized eligibility and launch requirements
**CDP parameters** | Adjust permitted fee and distribution splits for a pAsset instance | Hardcoded min/max bounds
**Conversion parameters** | Tune pETH-to-POLAR conversion settings (rebate, half-life, spike sensitivity) | Rebate bounded 0–100%; other bounds pending
**StablecoinOS onboarding** | Whitelist eligible participants into Flow revenue sharing | Must build on pETH/pAssets and meet published requirements
**Primary interest split** | Set how primary interest is split between the Stability Pool and POLAR stakers (remainder to the ecosystem fund) | Stability Pool and stakers each floored at 25% (hardcoded)
**Flow weights** | Set whitelisted recipient weights and related edge parameters | Hardcoded limits; no gauges, bribes, or emissions
**Treasury programs** | Operate programs such as the Polar Council | Initially team-managed until a framework is published

A stewardship vote can bring a new pAsset or Flow participant into the system, or move an approved parameter within its range. It cannot alter the bonding curve, redemption mechanism, liquidation logic, Stability Pool mechanics, or core contract immutability.

## What stewardship does not do

Polaris stewardship does **not**:

- Upgrade or replace immutable core contracts.
- Add admin keys, pause powers, freeze powers, or blacklists.
- Create POLAR emissions gauges.
- Route bribes or vote-buying markets through the protocol.
- Mint POLAR outside the pETH conversion mechanism.
- Set safety-critical parameters (such as interest-rate maxima, interest-split floors, and fee or rebate ceilings) outside their hardcoded bounds.
- Change a user's trove, Stability Pool deposit, pETH balance, or lock by discretion.

## Operator prerequisites

Teams seeking onboarding, a pAsset instance, Flow participation, or another stewarded action should expect to provide:

- Clear contract addresses once deployed and verified.
- A concise description of the product, risk surface, and user flow.
- The exact stewarded action requested.
- The parameter values requested, with the published min/max bounds shown beside them.
- Evidence that the action cannot touch immutable core logic.
- Security status: audits, tests, admin roles, upgradeability, and emergency controls for the requesting team's own contracts.
- Revenue-sharing, Flow, or integration assumptions, if relevant.
- Monitoring and reporting commitments after approval.

The final submission template is pending. Until it is published, operators should prepare around these fields rather than assume an informal path.

## Proposal and voting lifecycle

The final stewardship UI and contract-level process are pending. The intended lifecycle is:

Stage | Purpose

**Draft** | Describe the request, affected contracts, user impact, risks, and requested parameters
**Bounds check** | Confirm each requested value sits inside immutable min/max limits
**Security review** | Confirm verified source, audit status, admin roles, and integration risks
**Vote** | vePOLAR holders vote according to the published lock and vote-power rules
**Queue / execution** | Approved changes are queued and executed through the published contract path
**Verification** | Docs, indexers, and monitors confirm the new state onchain
**Monitoring** | Operators and stewards watch the result and propose reversals or adjustments if needed

No proposal is executable until the official process, contracts, and parameter bounds are published.

## The three-tier decision model

Polaris follows a simple ordering: solve a problem at the lowest tier that can solve it, and escalate only what the tier below cannot handle.

1. **Automate what you can.** Interest-rate setting, floor-price growth, liquidation processing, and redemptions run autonomously.
2. **Incentivize what you can't automate.** Where behavior can't be hardcoded but can be paid for, durable protocol revenue and Flow formulas handle it.
3. **Steward only what's left.** Only decisions that require judgment go to vePOLAR.

## Bounded blast radius

Stewards hold real power, so they can make real mistakes, but the immutable core caps the damage. A weak pAsset affects that instance, not the bonding curve or liquidation engine. A poor split can be moved back inside the same hardcoded bounds.

## How this compares to DAO governance

Dimension | Unbounded DAO governance | Polaris stewardship

**Scope** | Open-ended; can reach the base protocol | Enumerated and bounded
**Core** | Mutable via vote | Immutable, out of reach of any vote
**Incentives** | Often routed through emissions, gauges, and bribes | Routed through protocol revenue, configurable revenue splits, and Flows
**Failure mode** | Base-protocol denaturation, capture, extraction | Bounded mistakes at the edges

The lesson Polaris draws: bound the scope first. Less can be captured, overpaid for, or changed away from the base design.

## Structure over trust

Stewardship is not a promise to behave well; it is a structure with a bounded surface. The core is immutable. Each lever is bounded, auditable, and reversible where the underlying contract permits. Team influence is diluted through the [conversion mechanism](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) as new POLAR enters circulation.

---

[Stewardship, not Governance](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/stewardship-not-governance/)
Governance fails because of structure, not people. Here's how stewardship fixes the structure.

---

## Fee Router & Flows

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router.md

This page covers two related systems:

- **Fee Router:** internal revenue plumbing for protocol yield.
- **Flows:** formula-based revenue sharing for approved integrations.

> **Current status:** Fee Router addresses, ABIs, events, distribution cadence, Flow whitelists, and final parameter bounds are pending audited deployment. Use [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#contracts-and-addresses) and [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) for published values.

## Fee Router

The Fee Router receives protocol revenue, buffers it, and sends it to the configured recipient path. It moves existing assets only; it does not mint POLAR or create emissions.

### Inputs

Source | What enters

[Conversion auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) | Released ETH after any converter rebate, routed back into pETH yield paths
Bonding-curve swap fees | Fees from ETH/pETH activity, routed according to the final contract design
Other protocol revenue | Any additional source listed in the contract reference and yield docs

For the full source matrix, see [Yield Sources](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources).

### Outputs

Destination | What they receive

Troves | pETH yield credited through the relevant accounting path
POLAR stakers | A stewarded share of protocol revenue where staking is live
Flow recipients | Revenue-sharing distributions for whitelisted contracts

The Fee Router buffers incoming value before distribution. Buffering smooths bursty conversions and swap fees into steadier accounting periods.

The **converter rebate** sits upstream of the router. It controls how much released ETH returns to the converter before the rest becomes system yield. See [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions#the-converter-rebate).

## Operator checks

Before relying on the Fee Router, operators and indexers should verify:

- Router address, source, and ABI.
- Published input sources.
- Current recipient path and any min/max bounds.
- Distribution cadence and claim path.
- Events for deposits, buffering, distributions, claims, and parameter changes.
- Audit notes specific to router accounting.

Indexers should track inputs by source, buffered amounts before distribution, each distribution or claim, and the parameter values active during the distribution.

## Flows

Flows share protocol revenue with approved contracts that create durable demand for Polaris assets. They are designed to replace recurring token emissions with revenue paid in pETH or pAssets.

The base rule:

```text
Flow share = contract's weighted asset held / total weighted asset held across whitelisted contracts
```

If a whitelisted contract holds 20% of the whitelisted pETH supply, it receives 20% of that Flow before launch-specific adjustments.

## Flow families

Flow | Recipients | Funded by

pETH Flow | Eligible pETH-holding contracts | Bonding-curve and conversion-related revenue
pAsset Flow | Eligible pAsset-holding contracts | Borrower interest or other pAsset revenue after required Stability Pool allocation

Each Flow has its own whitelist, observed asset, recipient rules, and parameter bounds. Final contracts will define which assets, recipients, and revenue sources go live.

## Stewarded edges

The Flow formula should run automatically once configured. Stewardship is limited to edge decisions:

- Admit or remove a contract from a whitelist.
- Set a bounded weight or kickstart where allowed.
- Distinguish observed address from recipient address where contracts support it.
- Adjust entries inside published bounds.

Flow eligibility is not automatic. Operators should prepare product details, verified contracts, admin/upgrade model, requested Flow, monitoring plan, and user risk disclosure.

## Flow lifecycle

Stage | What happens

Application | Operator submits product, contracts, requested Flow, and risk details
Review | Stewards check eligibility, security posture, and bounds
Vote | vePOLAR votes on whitelist admission and settings
Activation | Recipient is added through the official execution path
Distribution | Formula allocates revenue by holdings, weights, and active parameters
Monitoring | Holdings, distributions, and risks are tracked

## Flows vs emissions

Aspect | Emission gauges | Polaris Flows

Currency | Governance token emissions | pETH or pAssets
Funding | Inflation | Protocol revenue
Allocation | Recurring votes and bribes | Formula based on eligible holdings
Stewardship load | Ongoing weight wars | Onboarding plus bounded adjustments

The only intended path for new POLAR after inception is [pETH conversion](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions). Flows use protocol revenue instead.

## Related docs

- [Yield Sources](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources) for where revenue originates.
- [POLAR Tokenomics](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics) for staking, vePOLAR, and pending economics.
- [Stewardship](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship) for the bounded decision model.

---

[Sustaining the Ecosystem: pETH & pUSD Flows](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/polaris-flows/)
How Polaris replaces emission-style incentives with an autonomous, hard-asset distribution system to sustain the pETH and pAssets ecosystems.

---

## vePOLAR Locking and Stewardship

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar.md

**vePOLAR is vote-escrowed POLAR.** Lock [POLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar) to receive stewardship rights for bounded parameters and edge decisions.

> **Current status:** vePOLAR is not the same as current **POLAR Staking**. The app may expose stake/unstake/claim actions for POLAR rewards on testnet, while the vePOLAR locking interface, contract address, lock durations, vote-power decay, unlock rules, proposal thresholds, and final yield split are pending. Do not lock POLAR through an unofficial interface.

## What vePOLAR does

```
lock POLAR -> receive vePOLAR -> steward bounded parameters + receive routed yield
```

vePOLAR holders are **stewards, not governors**. They steer quantitative parameters within hardcoded ranges and vote on permitted onboarding decisions. The bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, redemptions, and Stability Pool mechanics are outside vote scope.

vePOLAR holders can participate in decisions to:

- License new pAssets onto Polaris infrastructure.
- Onboard eligible participants into StablecoinOS and [Flow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router) revenue sharing.
- Set bounded interest splits and CDP parameters.
- Tune [conversion](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) parameters within their hardcoded range.
- Vote on treasury programs once the framework is published.

Each action is bounded, auditable, and limited to the surfaces in [Stewardship, not Governance](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship).

## POLAR Staking is separate

POLAR Staking is a staking/rewards surface: stake POLAR, unstake where allowed, and claim routed rewards. It does not by itself create vote-escrowed vePOLAR, lock-duration voting power, or stewardship rights.

vePOLAR is the future locking layer. Once live, this page will list the official lock contract, durations, vote-power formula, unlock rules, delegation, proposal thresholds, and yield claim path.

## What vePOLAR does not do

vePOLAR cannot upgrade core contracts; change pETH, CDP, liquidation, redemption, or Stability Pool mechanics; create gauges or bribe markets; direct POLAR emissions; mint POLAR outside the [conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions); or move any parameter outside its immutable min/max range.

## Lock status

The final lock design is pending. When vePOLAR is deployed, this page will list:

Field | Status

POLAR contract address | Pending
vePOLAR contract address | Pending
Network | Public Testnet 1: Sepolia; production: Ethereum mainnet after launch
Lock duration choices | 1 week to 4 years
Vote-power formula and decay | Linear decay
Increase/extend | Enabled
Withdraw | Only at lock expiry
Delegation | Enabled

## Yield for lockers

vePOLAR is intended to be more than influence alone. Lockers are expected to be one destination of distributed protocol yield, while current POLAR Staking is the app surface that can receive routed staking rewards where the deployment exposes it. The pETH the [Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router) distributes to troves is a separate stream.

The exact split, buffering, and claim mechanics are pending final deployment. This is protocol revenue, not POLAR emissions.

## Prerequisites for locking

Before locking POLAR, verify:

- The official app URL.
- POLAR and vePOLAR addresses against [Contracts and Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#contracts-and-addresses).
- Verified source and ABI against [Contracts and Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#contracts-and-addresses).
- Lock duration, voting power, unlock, and withdrawal rules.
- The final yield split and claim path.
- The audit status of the locking and stewardship contracts.

Locking is an illiquid commitment by design. Do not lock through any contract or frontend not published in official Polaris docs.

## The Polar Council

The **Polar Council** is a treasury-funded program to cultivate aligned, active stewards. See [The Polar Council](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/tokenomics#the-polar-council) for its scope and pending terms.

---

## Why Polaris Finance

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/why-polaris
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/why-polaris.md

## The trilemma

Stablecoins are usually said to be **scalable, yield-bearing, or trustless: pick two**. Market structure mostly reinforces that framing: the largest stablecoins rely on issuers, custodians, offchain assets, or other counterparties, while cleaner trustless designs stay smaller.

That trade-off is real, but it is not a law of nature. It exists because, for years, high offchain interest rates made it *easy* to back a stablecoin with T-bills and CEX yield and bring that yield onchain. Easy, but not trustless; and not innovative.

Image: Vitalik's three challenges for decentralized stablecoins — a better-than-USD index, a capture-resistant oracle, and competing with staking yield — mapped to the Polaris approach, with the oracle surface marked partial

## The degradation pattern

The deeper problem is **structural drift toward centralization**. Stablecoins tend to start relatively clean and get dirtier in how they source yield as they grow, because centralized backing is the path of least resistance to scale.

Image: Only a sliver of the roughly $339B stablecoin market is non-freezable and trustlessly issued: most supply (USDC, USDT, USDS) carries issuer freeze or blacklist power, while counterparty-free names like LUSD and BOLD are a tiny fraction — with pUSD marked as forthcoming

Feature | **USDC** | **USDS / DAI** | **USDe** | **LUSD / BOLD** | **pUSD**

Issuer dependency | Centralized issuer with freeze/blacklist | Governance-set rates, RWA exposure | Market-neutral, custody-dependent | Counterparty-free | Counterparty-free
Yield source | Offchain reserves (not passed to holders) | Mixed: crypto + RWA + governance | Counterparty-dependent infrastructure | Limited onchain sources | Onchain protocol activity
Scalability constraint | Banking relationships | Governance decisions | Counterparty capacity | Capital inefficiency | Bonding-curve scaling
Immutable core | No | Partial | No | Yes | Yes

## The counterparty-free lineage

Polaris descends from the line of immutable, counterparty-free CDP stablecoins, built to fix the limitation each prior generation hit.

Image: Polaris lineage: SAI in 2017, LUSD in 2021, BOLD and Liquity V2 public code and testnet in 2024 with mainnet in 2025, and pUSD in 2026

*The counterparty-free CDP lineage, 2017 to present. The BOLD/Liquity V2 milestone spans 2024 public code/testnet work and 2025 mainnet launch.*

In text, the lineage reads:

1. **2017 — SAI:** the first CDP stablecoin, single-collateral. It later upgraded to DAI and then drifted toward centralized backing.
2. **2021 — LUSD:** immutable and decentralized, but capital-inefficient, with long over-peg periods; exact percentages depend on the dataset, date range, and sampling cadence.
3. **2024/2025 — BOLD (Liquity V2):** 2024 public code/testnet work followed by Liquity's May 2025 mainnet launch — a refined CDP mechanism from the original Liquity developers, still trustless, still yield-constrained.
4. **2026 — pUSD:** the next chapter. A bonding-curve collateral design that solves the overpeg problem and unlocks onchain-scaling yield without adding a counterparty.

## How Polaris resolves it

Polaris is designed so the main dependencies are immutable code and Ethereum:

- **Onchain-only yield.** Every yield source — borrower interest, bonding curve swap fees, conversion gains — is internal to the protocol and grows with activity. Nothing is rented from a CEX or T-bill desk.
- **Immutable core.** The bonding curve, CDP logic, liquidations, redemptions, and the Stability Pool are intended to be fixed in audited deployment code. No admin key can add a custodial PSM, external-collateral module, or freeze function under pressure.
- **Counterparty-free by construction.** pUSD sits in the same counterparty-free lineage as LUSD and BOLD — but adds yield that *scales* with DeFi volume rather than capping growth at what a counterparty can deliver.

Polaris turns onchain activity into yield through its bonding curve, and extends the model to other assets through [StablecoinOS](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts).

Next steps:
- [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts): A one-page map of every key term in the Polaris system.
- [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status): Official app and network status, plus the shortest path from "I have ETH" to using Polaris.
- [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting): How the CDP works from first principles.

---

[Why Polaris?](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/why-polaris/)
Analyzing where USDC, USDS, USDe, and LUSD fall short — and how pUSD delivers scalable, yield-bearing stability without counterparty risk.

---

## Polaris Stability Pool

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield.md

The Stability Pool is a **first-loss pool**. When a trove is liquidated, the pool burns pAssets to cover the debt and receives pETH collateral in return. Your deposit can be partially consumed and replaced by pETH. This is the mechanism, not an edge case.

## What the Stability Pool does

The Stability Pool keeps Polaris solvent. It holds pAsset deposits that are used to absorb liquidated trove debt. In exchange, depositors receive the pETH collateral seized from liquidated troves, usually at a discount to market price. This creates an alignment of incentives: depositors profit from liquidations, which keeps the system healthy.

Image: How the Stability Pool fits the system: ETH enters the bonding curve as pETH, which collateralizes CDPs that mint pUSD; pUSD deposited in the Stability Pool absorbs liquidated debt and earns discounted pETH plus a share of borrower interest

## Where the yield comes from

Depositors earn from two streams:

1. **Borrower interest.** A share of the pAsset-denominated interest paid by all troves borrowing that pAsset routes to the pool. This is the steady-state base yield.
2. **Liquidation gains.** When a trove is liquidated, the pool absorbs its debt (burning depositors' pAssets) and receives its pETH collateral. The collateral value at the moment of liquidation typically exceeds the absorbed debt, so the net position improves — but the composition shifts from pAssets to pETH.

The interest share is the steady base; liquidation gains spike when markets are turbulent. Together they make the Stability Pool the protocol's primary yield venue for pAssets. Current split values live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters).

## First-loss mechanics

When a liquidation occurs:

- The pool burns enough pAssets to match the trove's debt.
- The pool receives the trove's pETH collateral.
- Every depositor's pAsset balance shrinks proportionally; every depositor's pETH balance grows proportionally.

**Before depositing:** your share can be drawn down at any time when any trove carrying that pAsset's debt crosses the liquidation line. The pool does not protect individuals from this; it distributes it equally. The risk is priced into the liquidation discount — on average, the pETH you receive is worth more than the pAssets you gave up — but realized gain depends on ETH price movement between the liquidation and when you exit.

## Wrapper vaults

Raw Stability Pool mechanics leave depositors holding pETH after liquidations. Wrapper vaults are an anticipated abstraction above the raw pool, but they are not a live product and details are not published.

## Risk

The Stability Pool is the protocol's *intended* first-loss layer. Key risks:

- **pETH price exposure.** Liquidation gains arrive as pETH, which is volatile against ETH. A large liquidation event that converts your full deposit to pETH, followed by a pETH price drop, can leave you worse off in ETH terms.
- **Extreme drawdown.** If a large fraction of all troves are liquidated simultaneously (e.g., a flash crash before the liquidation engine processes them), the pool may not fully cover all debt. In that case the protocol falls back to redistribution — redistributing remaining collateral and debt across surviving troves — and the pool is partially or fully depleted.
- **Smart-contract risk.** The pool is a smart contract; a bug can affect any depositor. Mitigated by audits, never zero.

In a severe or flash crash, a trove can fall below 100% collateralization before being liquidated. In that case the pool absorbs more debt than the collateral it receives is worth. That underwater liquidation loss is shared proportionally by all depositors.

Your liquidation gains are also denominated in pETH, which is [volatile against ETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price). The wrapper vaults above mitigate that exposure, but at the base layer you accept pETH.

## How to deposit

You need a pAsset to deposit, usually **pUSD** ([mint some](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/open-a-trove) if you hold none), plus ETH for gas. Re-read the pre-deposit warning before signing.

### Open the pool

Open the Polaris app and go to the **Stability Pool** for your pAsset (e.g. the pUSD pool).

### Enter a deposit amount

Enter the amount of pUSD to deposit.

### Approve the spend

**Approve** the pUSD spend, if prompted (a one-time token approval).

### Confirm the deposit

Confirm the **deposit** transaction in your wallet.

## Related docs

- [Yield Sources](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources) for every yield stream on Polaris.
- [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) for how troves are closed and the pool is triggered.
- [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap) for the bundled WETH → pool flow.
- [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure) before treating yield as principal-safe.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Stability Pool, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Polaris Yield Sources

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield/yield-sources.md

Polaris yield comes from onchain protocol activity: borrowing, liquidations, swaps, and conversions. This page lists the sources; [Fee Router & Flows](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router) covers routing.

> Adoption ⇒ activity ⇒ velocity ⇒ fees ⇒ yield ⇒ more demand.

## The sources

Source | Where it originates | Flows to

**Borrower interest** | Interest [trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove) owners pay on minted pAssets | Primarily [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield) depositors, with stewarded splits where configured
**Liquidation gains** | The penalty on liquidated troves, paid in discounted pETH | Stability Pool depositors
**Bonding curve swap fees** | Fees on every ETH ⇄ pETH swap, buffered by the Fee Router and later floor-sold and burned to raise the [floor price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price) | pETH holders / troves
**Conversion gains** | ETH released when pETH is burned to mint POLAR in a [conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions) | pETH holders / troves

The first two drive [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield) yield and are the primary venue for pAsset holders. The bonding curve and conversion components make **pETH used as trove collateral yield-bearing** — the yield accrues to troves and to pETH's rising floor, not to a passive token balance — which is why using pETH as collateral can lower a borrower's net effective cost.

## Why this is "self-scaling"

These sources depend on protocol **activity**:

- More troves opened ⇒ more borrower interest.
- More volatility ⇒ more liquidation gains.
- More trading ⇒ more bonding curve swap fees.
- More POLAR minted ⇒ more conversion gains.

Yield scales with pAsset supply and protocol use, but realized returns still depend on activity, rates, liquidations, contract execution, and market conditions.

## Owned, not rented

Polaris yield is not subsidized, bridged in, or voted into being. It emerges from protocol use and is captured by the protocol itself.

## How yield is distributed

Conversion proceeds and swap fees go through the **[Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router)**. Borrower interest follows separate pAsset-denominated split logic. Current values and pending production status live on [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters).

## Parameters

Use [Parameters](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#parameters) for current yield-related values. This page owns the source model, not numeric tables.

---

[pUSD: No Counterparty, No Ceiling](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/pusd-no-counterparty/)
How pUSD delivers counterparty-free yield that scales with protocol adoption — and the ecosystem taking shape around it.

Relevant app/search vocabulary: Earn, earn, yield, APR, Stability Pool, deposit, claim rewards.

---

## Polaris Glossary

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources.md

Every key Polaris term, alphabetized. Each entry is one or two sentences and links to its full treatment where one exists. For the narrative map of how the pieces fit, read [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts).

## Live app terms

These labels match the current testnet app and route users to the glossary entry plus the matching guide page.

App term | Glossary entry | Guide

Advanced | [Advanced](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#a) | [Advanced app tools](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/advanced)
APR | [APR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#a) | [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn)
Borrow / CDP | [CDP](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#c), [Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#t) | [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow)
Earn / SP | [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#s), [SP](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#s) | [Earn](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/earn)
Floor Carry | [Floor Carry](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#f) | [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split)
fpETH / vpETH | [fpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#f), [vpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#v) | [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split)
Guide | [Guide](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#g) | [Polaris App](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app)
LTV | [LTV](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#l) | [Managing Your Trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/managing-your-trove)
POLAR Staking | [POLAR Staking](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#p) | [POLAR token](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar)
PSM / Redemption | [PSM](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#p), [Redemption](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#r) | [Advanced](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/advanced)
Reserve Loan | [Reserve Loan](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#r) | [Borrow](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/borrow)
Split | [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#s) | [Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/split)
Swap / WETH | [WETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#w), [pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#p) | [Swap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/swap)
Zap | [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#z) | [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap)

## A-Z jump

[A](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#a) · [B](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#b) · [C](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#c) · [F](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#f) · [G](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#g) · [I](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#i) · [L](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#l) · [M](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#m) · [O](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#o) · [P](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#p) · [R](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#r) · [S](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#s) · [T](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#t) · [V](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#v) · [W](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#w) · [Z](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#z)

## A

**Advanced**
The app area for expert, permissionless operations: PSM mint/redeem of pUSD and pGOLD, pETH-to-POLAR conversion, and the testnet faucet. Use it only after verifying the contract, function, token, amount, and quote; see [Advanced](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/advanced).

**APR (Annual Percentage Rate)**
An annualized rate estimate, commonly shown for borrow cost or yield. APR is not a promised return: realized cost or yield can change with rates, activity, liquidations, fees, execution, and market conditions.

## B

**Bonding curve**
The two-way curve that mints and burns [pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth), giving it a contract-native buy/sell path with no liquidity providers. See [pETH Bonding Curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth) and [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price).

## C

**CDP (Collateralized Debt Position)**
The mechanism behind minting: post collateral, mint a pAsset directly from the protocol rather than borrowing another user's funds. Each position is an isolated [trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#t). See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).

**Conversion auction**
The mechanism that burns [pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth) to mint new [POLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar). See [Conversion Auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions).

**Converter rebate**
A stewardship-controlled percentage of ETH returned to the converter in a [conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions). The dial between conversion volume and system yield.

## F

**Fee Router**
Receives protocol revenue, buffers it, and routes it to configured yield paths. See [Fee Router & Flows](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/fee-router).

**Floor Carry**
The possible return from holding [fpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#f) while the pETH [floor price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#f) rises. It is not a fixed APR or a guarantee; it depends on actual floor growth, market pricing, execution costs, and the redemption path remaining available.

**Floor price**
The contract-defined minimum price of [pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth), in ETH terms, under the bonding-curve assumptions. See [pETH Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price).

**Floor ratio**
The floor price divided by the market price, as a percentage. It frames the curve-implied drawdown before gas, slippage, MEV, smart-contract risk, and chain-liveness risk. See [The Floor Price](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/floor-price).

**Flows**
The protocol's internal value movements (fees, conversions, yield distribution), routed by the [Fee Router](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#f). Inward-facing flows sustain the core; outward-facing flows grow the ecosystem (see *Inward-facing / Outward-facing*).

**fpETH**
The floor-price leg created when [pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) is split into fpETH and [vpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#v). fpETH can be redeemed for WETH at the pETH floor-price value under the splitter and bonding-curve assumptions, and it is used as reserve-loan collateral.

## G

**Guide**
The app area that routes users by goal, such as getting pETH, borrowing pAssets, earning yield, splitting pETH, or verifying safety. See [Polaris App](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app).

## I

**ICR (Individual Collateralization Ratio)**
The protocol-side inverse of [LTV](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#l): pETH collateral value divided by pAsset debt. The app shows LTV; if ICR falls below the minimum, the equivalent LTV has crossed the maximum and the trove can be [liquidated](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).

**Inward-facing / Outward-facing**
The design split between core mechanics handled by pETH/pAssets and ecosystem growth handled by [POLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar).

## L

**LTV (loan-to-value)**
The app-facing health metric for a [trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#t): pAsset debt divided by pETH collateral value. Lower LTV is safer; if LTV exceeds the maximum, the trove can be [liquidated](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).

**Liquidation**
The process that closes an undercollateralized [trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#t): when its [LTV](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#l) exceeds the maximum, the [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield) absorbs its debt and receives its collateral — or, if the pool is too small, the debt and collateral redistribute to other troves. See [Liquidations](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations).

## M

**Minting (mechanism)**
The inverse of [redemption](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#r): when a pAsset trades *above* peg (e.g. pUSD > \$1), arbitrageurs add pETH to troves to mint the pAsset and sell it, pushing the price down. This restores the ceiling and is one of the onchain signals used to set interest rates. See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).

**Minting (verb)**
Creating a pAsset by opening a [trove](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#t) and drawing pAsset debt against pETH collateral. Polaris *mints* pAssets, not "issues" or "creates" them.

## O

**Oracle**
An onchain feed used to price ETH collateral. Peg awareness uses minting and redemption volume as internal signals; address status lives on [Contracts & Addresses](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/testnet#contracts-and-addresses).

## P

**pAsset**
A Polaris synthetic debt asset minted from a CDP — pUSD, pGOLD, pCHF, and beyond. Each is overcollateralized by pETH and counterparty-free. pETH itself is the collateral token, not CDP-minted pAsset debt. See [The pAsset Catalog](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog).

**pBigMac**
A novelty pAsset demonstrating the "mint anything" thesis: if a price feed exists, Polaris can mint a pAsset that tracks it.

**pCHF**
The Polaris synthetic Swiss franc — a pAsset pegged to CHF.

**pETH**
ETH deposited into the two-way [bonding curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth), and the only collateral Polaris accepts for pAsset troves. See [pETH Bonding Curve](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth).

**pGOLD**
The Polaris synthetic gold pAsset, designed to track gold without issuer custody or KYC at the base protocol layer. See [The pAsset Catalog](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/passet-catalog).

**Pathfinders**
Early Polaris contributors and community members selected through public contribution paths.

**Polar Council**
A planned treasury-funded program for onboarding and ecosystem contributors. Terms are pending publication.

**POLAR**
The protocol's stewardship token and outward-facing growth lever. After the initial distribution, new POLAR can be minted *only* by burning pETH in a [conversion auction](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions). See [The POLAR Token](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar).

**POLAR Staking**
The current app surface for staking POLAR and claiming routed rewards where exposed. It is distinct from forthcoming [vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#v) locking.

**PSM (Peg Stability Module)**
The contract/module that handles pAsset minting and redemption mechanics for a pAsset instance. Use PSM or redemption actions only after checking the pAsset, fee, output amount, and contract address.

**pUSD**
The flagship pAsset, designed to track the US dollar and be backed by pETH collateral. See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).

## R

**Recovery Mode**
A protective state triggered when a pAsset's system-wide reserve-to-debt ratio falls below a threshold. It restricts risk-increasing borrower actions; liquidation eligibility is unchanged. See [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode).

**Redemption**
Exchanging a pAsset for its underlying collateral at face value (minus a fee) when the pAsset trades *below* peg. Redemptions restore the floor and are one of the onchain signals used to set interest rates. See [Redemptions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations).

**Reserve Loan**
A WETH loan against [fpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#f) floor-value collateral. It is separate from a pAsset trove: reserve loans use the pETH floor leg and reserve accounting, while troves mint pAssets against pETH collateral.

## S

**sepETH**
Sepolia ETH, the testnet-native gas asset used on Public Testnet 1. It has no mainnet ETH value; when wrapped for app flows it may appear as WETH.

**SP (Stability Pool)**
Common abbreviation for the [Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#s), the pAsset first-loss pool that absorbs liquidations and pays depositors borrower-interest share and liquidation gains.

**Split**
The app flow and contract operation that turns pETH into equal amounts of [fpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#f) and [vpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#v), or merges equal fpETH and vpETH amounts back into pETH. See [pETH Split](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split).

**Spike-and-decay**
The pricing model for [conversion auctions](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/polar/conversion-auctions): the conversion price continuously decays until a conversion *spikes* it upward, proportionally to the POLAR supply that conversion inflated.

**Stability Pool**
A reserve of pAssets that absorbs the debt of [liquidated](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/liquidations) troves and is the protocol's primary venue for earning yield. See [The Stability Pool](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield).

**StablecoinOS**
The CDP-factory layer: many distinct pAssets, each issued by its own CDP instance, all sharing one pETH collateral and bonding curve. See [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts).

**Stewardship**
The bounded role stewards play: tuning *quantitative parameters within the bounds the contracts define*. The immutable core cannot be changed; stewards steward, they do not govern. See [Stewardship, not Governance](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship).

## T

**TCR (Total Collateralization Ratio)**
The system-wide reserve-to-debt health metric for a pAsset. Dropping below the configured threshold triggers [Recovery Mode](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations/recovery-mode).

**Trove**
An individual borrowing position in a Polaris CDP. Each trove is isolated, with its own collateral, debt, and [LTV](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#l). Polaris uses *trove*, never "vault" or "position". See [Minting pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting).

## V

**vePOLAR**
The forthcoming vote-escrow layer for POLAR, used for [stewardship](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship) rights and a yield path. See [vePOLAR](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship/vepolar).

**vpETH**
The volatility-premium leg created when [pETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/peth/split) is split into [fpETH](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources#f) and vpETH. vpETH represents exposure to pETH's market value above the floor and can lose significant value if the premium compresses.

## W

**WETH**
Wrapped ETH, the ERC-20 form of ETH used by app contracts and testnet flows. On Sepolia, WETH is a test token backed by testnet ETH, not mainnet ETH.

## Z

**Zap**
An app flow that bundles multiple steps, such as WETH -> pUSD or pGOLD -> Stability Pool deposit. A zap is an execution shortcut, not a risk reducer; check approvals, simulation, slippage, quote freshness, and first-loss risk. See [Zap](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/zap).

---

Missing a term? The narrative version of every concept lives on its dedicated page, starting from [Core Concepts](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/core-concepts); the long-form essays are on the [Polaris blog](https://polarisfinance.io/blog/).

Relevant app/search vocabulary: app, official app, testnet, Sepolia, WETH, WETH faucet, Zap, Split, Swap, Borrow, Earn, Guide, Advanced, Analytics, APR, Reserve Loan.

---

## Polaris Audits & Security

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/audits-security
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/audits-security.md

> **Pre-launch note:** Audits and verifications are ongoing; this page will be updated with major developments.

## The security model

Polaris's intended security model rests as much on what it *cannot* do as on what it can:

- **Immutability.** The core contracts are intended to be non-upgradeable after deployment. This removes upgrade-key and proxy-admin attack surface, raising the stakes on getting the code right: immutability cuts both ways — it cannot be tampered with, and it cannot be patched.
- **No admin keys.** The design has no freeze, blacklist, or pause functions for anyone, including the team. No privileged key is intended to move user funds.
- **Bounded stewardship.** Stewards adjust only quantitative parameters — many of them bounded by hardcoded ranges (for example interest-rate maxima, fee-split floors, and the mint/redemption decay factors) — and cannot reach the core logic. See [Stewardship, not Governance](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/stewardship).
- **Narrow oracle dependency.** Peg awareness uses internal redemption and minting volume rather than a pAsset price oracle. Collateral pricing still requires an ETH price feed; the provider, feed address, and network details are not published until launch.

For the full accounting of what can still go wrong, read the [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

---

## Polaris Brand Assets

URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/brand-assets
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/brand-assets.md

Everything you need to represent Polaris correctly: logos, palette, and type. The canonical surface is dark: Polaris was designed against a night sky, not a white page.

Image: Polaris full logo

*Full logo preview for placement checks; keep the SVG unmodified and provide enough contrast around it.*

## Logos

The logo files live at the site root and link directly:

Asset | Path | Use

Emblem | `/emblem.svg` | Standalone mark, for avatars, favicons, tight spaces
Full logo | `/full-logo.svg` | Mark plus wordmark, for headers and primary placements
Favicon (SVG) | `/favicon.svg` | Scalable browser favicon
Favicon (PNG / ICO) | `/favicon.png`, `/favicon.ico` | Raster favicons for legacy contexts
Apple touch icon | `/apple-touch-icon.png` | iOS home-screen icon

## Palette — Celestial Night Sky

### Navy backgrounds
